## Screen
A date selection step within a multi-step search/booking flow for a rental/travel marketplace. This is a modal-style overlay that sits mid-funnel — after location entry, before guest count. The layout is a vertical stack: category tabs at top, a completed "Where" summary row, an expanded "When" card with inline calendar, and a fixed bottom action bar.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in distinct card-per-step logic — each search parameter gets its own elevated white card. The active step ("When") is visually dominant with generous internal padding and a large question-format label. The "Where" card above it is collapsed/summary state, creating a clear visual hierarchy of done vs. in-progress. The calendar scrolls vertically within the card, with month labels acting as section headers. Bottom action bar floats fixed with a frosted/gradient fade above it to hint at scrollable content beneath.

---

## Components
- **Category tab bar** — Icon + label tabs, emoji icons, active state uses bold text + underline (not a pill or background fill). Subtle and text-forward.
- **Summary row card** — Rounded rectangle, white fill, light shadow. Left-aligned label ("Where") in muted gray, right-aligned value in dark text. Clean two-column label/value pattern.
- **Date mode segmented control** — Pill-shaped container with three options: Dates / Months / Flexible. Active option ("Dates") uses a white filled pill with subtle shadow inside a light gray track. Inset shadow on track gives it a recessed feel.
- **Calendar grid** — Standard 7-column grid. Past/unavailable dates rendered in light gray, available dates in full black. Month name as a left-aligned medium-weight label above each grid.
- **Date flexibility chips** — Horizontal scroll row at bottom of card: "Exact dates," "+ 1 day," "+ 2 days" — pill-shaped, outlined, compact. Allows range softening without a separate screen.
- **Bottom action bar** — "Reset" as underlined text link (left), "Next" as a large dark filled rounded button (right). High contrast CTA.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Large bold question heading ("When?") → medium month labels → small day-of-week headers in gray → date numerals in black or muted gray
- Question-format headings ("When?") create a conversational tone rather than a clinical form label
- **Color palette**: Near-white background (#F5F5F5 range), pure white cards, dark charcoal for CTA and active text, medium gray for inactive/past states
- Color used functionally: gray = unavailable/past, black = selectable, white pill = active segment selection

---

## Patterns
- **Progressive disclosure via card stacking** — Completed steps collapse into summary rows; current step expands fully. Keeps the form feeling lightweight even when complex.
- **Question-as-label** — Using "When?" instead of "Select dates" shifts the tone to conversational UX, reducing cognitive friction.
- **Flexibility modifier chips** — The "+ 1 day / + 2 days" row is a clever middle ground between exact date picking and fully flexible mode. Reduces abandonment from users unsure of exact dates.
- **Vertical scrolling calendar vs. month-by-month pagination** — Continuous scroll with month headers feels more natural for range selection; avoids the tap-to-advance friction of paginated calendars.
- **Fade mask on scroll edge** — Gradient at the bottom of the card signals more content below without requiring a "show more" button.
- **Underline-only reset** — Using a text link for destructive/secondary action rather than a ghost button keeps visual weight on the primary CTA.

---

## Notes
- Question-format step labels → conversational form pattern, worth stealing for any multi-step flow
- Segmented control with inset shadow track = more premium feel than flat toggle
- Collapse-to-summary for completed steps = great for long search forms
- Flexibility chips = smart UX for date uncertainty, consider for any booking flow
- Continuous vertical calendar scroll > paginated for range picking
- Emoji icons in tab bar = personality without heavy illustration overhead
- Float CTA bar + fade mask = standard but executed cleanly here

## Screen
Date selection step in a multi-step search/booking flow for a short-term rental or experience booking app. This is a stepped modal/sheet that overlays the main search interface. The layout stacks a category tab bar at top, a completed "where" summary row, and an expanded "when" card containing the calendar. Bottom bar holds Reset + Next actions.

## Layout & Spacing
Two distinct card zones: a compact summary card (Where, already answered) and a large active card (When, currently in focus). The active card takes dominant vertical space, signaling user attention. Calendar content is generously spaced — day cells have ample tap targets. Month labels are left-aligned with significant top padding, creating clear visual breaks between months. Bottom pill-row for date flexibility options is partially clipped, hinting at scrollability.

## Components
- **Category tab bar**: Three tabs with emoji icons + labels; active tab underlined, not pill-highlighted — subtle but clear
- **Close button**: Circular white button with × — floats at top right, isolated from tab group
- **Summary row card**: White rounded card, label left / value right — minimal, read-only state
- **"When?" header**: Large bold question-format heading inside the active card
- **Segmented toggle (Dates / Months / Flexible)**: Pill-shaped container; active option is white pill with shadow, inactive options are plain text — classic iOS-style segmented control
- **Calendar grid**: Day-of-week headers in gray, month name as section header, past dates rendered in light gray (disabled), available dates in black
- **Flexibility chips**: Pill-shaped outline chips at bottom — "Exact dates," "+ 1 day," "+ 2 days" — horizontally scrollable row
- **Bottom action bar**: "Reset" as underlined text link (left), "Next" as large dark filled rounded button (right)

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: "When?" is the largest, boldest element (~28–32px, heavy weight) → Month names are medium-weight section labels → Day numbers are regular weight → Day-of-week headers are small gray caps
- **Color palette**: Near-white background (#F5F5F5), white cards, black for active text and CTA button, medium gray for disabled/past dates and inactive labels
- **Functional color use**: Gray = unavailable/past; black = selectable/active; white pill = selected state in segmented control; dark button = primary forward action

## Patterns
- **Conversational question as header** — "When?" instead of "Select dates" makes the UI feel like a dialogue, reducing cognitive formality
- **Progressive disclosure via card stacking** — completed steps collapse into compact summary rows; current step expands fully. Teaches users where they are without a progress bar
- **Segmented control with shadow-pill active state** — the white raised pill on gray background is more tactile than a simple underline or color change; implies physical selection
- **Flexibility modifier chips below calendar** — separating "exact dates" vs. "+1 day" flexibility as a post-selection modifier is smart UX; doesn't complicate the calendar itself
- **Past dates visible but muted** — showing unavailable dates in gray rather than hiding them preserves calendar spatial orientation; users understand the grid without gaps
- **Asymmetric bottom actions** — Reset as text link (low visual weight, destructive/secondary) vs. Next as filled button (high weight, primary) creates clear action hierarchy without a second full button
- **Partial clip on flexibility row** — intentional truncation signals horizontal scroll without an explicit affordance

## Notes
- Question-format section headers = conversational tone trick, steal this
- Collapsed "done" steps as summary rows = accordion-lite pattern for multi-step forms
- Shadow-pill segmented control > underline for tactile feel
- Flexibility chips as calendar modifier layer = clean separation of concerns
- Gray-not-hidden past dates = preserve spatial context
- Text link Reset vs. filled Next = weight-based action hierarchy
- Partial row clip = scroll affordance without arrows or indicators
- Emoji in tab icons adds warmth without being childish at this scale

## Screen
Language learning app, translation exercise screen. Mid-lesson flow where user constructs a translated sentence by tapping word tiles. Sits within an active lesson session. Layout divides into three zones: progress/header, content/prompt area, and interactive answer-building zone with a CTA at bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Three clear horizontal bands: (1) progress bar + close, (2) illustrated prompt card, (3) word bank split by two thin divider lines into "selected answer" zone and "available words" zone. The two hairline dividers are the key spatial device — they create an implicit drop zone without labeling it. Generous vertical breathing room between the character illustration and word tiles. Bottom CTA is pinned with comfortable margin.

## Components
- **Progress bar**: Thin, rounded, green fill on gray track — minimal but always visible
- **Character illustration + speech bubble**: Floating illustrated character left-aligned; speech bubble right with rounded corners, subtle shadow, audio icon inline with text
- **Word tiles (active)**: White background, light gray border, rounded corners (~8px), medium weight text — feel tactile and pressable
- **Word tiles (used/ghost)**: Same shape but filled solid light gray — placeholder silhouettes showing where words were, preserving spatial layout of the bank
- **Answer zone**: Two horizontal rules creating an implicit "drop here" area with no explicit label
- **CHECK button**: Full-width, rounded rectangle, bold green, all-caps label — high contrast, unmissable

## Typography & Color
- Instruction text ("Translate this sentence"): Bold, ~18–20px, dark near-black
- Speech bubble text: Regular weight, ~16px, dark — readable at a glance
- Word tile text: Medium weight, ~15px
- Color palette: White background, green (#58CC02 range) for progress and CTA, blue for audio icon, gray for ghost tiles and progress track, warm neutrals in illustration
- Green used exclusively for progress and primary action — creates strong semantic association: green = go/correct

## Patterns
- **Ghost tile placeholders**: Used words leave gray silhouette shapes in the word bank — preserves grid layout, prevents reflow, and gives visual feedback on what's been "consumed" without collapsing the space. Elegant solution to a tricky state management UX problem.
- **Implicit answer drop zone**: Two thin lines with empty space between them signal "build your answer here" with zero explicit instruction — learned convention made spatial
- **Inline audio affordance**: Speaker icon sits inside the speech bubble text rather than as a separate button — reduces UI chrome, feels conversational
- **Character as prompt delivery mechanism**: Illustrated persona delivers the sentence, adding warmth and narrative context to what would otherwise be dry text
- **Word bank uses organic wrapping layout**: Tiles aren't forced into a rigid grid — they wrap naturally, making the bank feel like a scattered word pile rather than a table
- **CTA disabled-but-present**: CHECK button appears active green even before answer is complete — invites tapping, likely validates and prompts if incomplete rather than being grayed out

## Notes
- Ghost tiles = brilliant spatial memory trick, steal this
- Two-zone split via hairlines only — no labels needed if convention is established early
- Character + speech bubble = humanizes rote drill tasks
- Full-width green CTA always visible = reduces friction, no hunting
- Audio icon inline with content, not separate — less chrome
- Progress bar near-full = motivational, user sees they're almost done
- Warm illustration palette contrasts with clean white UI — makes content zone feel alive

## Screen
Home dashboard of a multi-currency fintech/money transfer app. This is the primary landing screen after login — the user's financial hub showing balances, recent transactions, and promotional content. Layout is a single scrollable column with a sticky bottom nav.

## Layout & Spacing
Top bar holds avatar + promotional pill + utility icon in a tight horizontal row. Below, a large bold greeting with an inline analytics icon. Quick-action pills sit just beneath. The balance section uses a horizontal scroll card carousel — the active card is fully visible, the next card (dashed border) peeks in from the right, signaling swipeability. Transactions section uses a clean list with generous row height. A promotional feature card anchors the bottom of the visible scroll area. Consistent ~20px horizontal margins throughout.

## Components
- **Avatar with notification dot** — initials-based avatar, small red dot overlay for unread state
- **Referral/promo pill** — green rounded pill with white text, high contrast CTA embedded inline in the header
- **Balance card** — rounded rect, light gray fill, flag emoji as currency identifier, masked account number with bank icon, large numeric balance display
- **"New" dashed card** — dashed border treatment distinguishes additive/empty state from active cards; + icon in circle
- **Quick-action pills** — "Send" is filled green (primary), "Add money" and "Request" are outlined/ghost — clear visual hierarchy among actions
- **Transaction row** — circular + icon (indicating credit), two-line label/date, right-aligned amount in green with secondary conversion amount below
- **Promotional banner card** — dark maroon/rich background, colorful illustrative graphic, dismissible via X button in circle overlay

## Typography & Color
Strong type hierarchy: greeting is bold ~28px, section headers ("Transactions," "Introducing Interest") are bold ~20px, body/labels are regular ~14px. Balance figure is the largest text on screen (~32px bold), drawing immediate attention. 

Color palette: white backgrounds, light gray card fills, **lime/yellow-green** as the primary brand action color (pill, Send button, positive transaction amount). Dark near-black for primary text. Red used sparingly for notification dot only. The promotional card introduces a deep maroon as an accent — contained and non-disruptive.

## Patterns
- **Peek-and-scroll carousel** — the dashed "New" card is intentionally half-visible, teaching swipe affordance without any instructional text
- **Dashed border as empty/additive state** — visually distinct from active cards while maintaining layout consistency; communicates "slot available"
- **Inline referral CTA in header** — the green pill earns prime real estate without a modal or banner interrupt; feels native rather than promotional
- **Dual-amount transaction display** — shows both the delta (+30 SGD) and the resulting balance (31.41 SGD) in the same row, reducing the need to do mental math
- **Primary action differentiation via fill** — among three sibling action pills, only one is filled; the others are ghost. Communicates frequency/importance hierarchy without disabling anything
- **Dismissible promo card with X overlay** — the close button sits inside the card as a floating circle, keeping the card visually clean while still offering an escape
- **Analytics icon inline with greeting** — subtle chart icon next to the welcome text offers a secondary utility path without cluttering the action area

## Notes
- Green pill in header = referral hook that doesn't feel like an ad — steal this placement
- Dashed border empty state card in carousel is elegant — no "empty state illustration" needed
- Balance card: flag as currency marker is faster to parse than text labels
- Two-line amount (delta + running total) in transaction rows = reduces cognitive load
- Ghost siblings to a filled primary CTA — better than icon-only or equal-weight buttons
- Dark themed promo card within light UI = visual contrast that draws eye without breaking system
- Pagination dots top-right of screen suggest this is part of an onboarding or feature tour overlay

## Screen
Home dashboard of a multi-currency fintech/money transfer app. This is the primary landing screen after login — the financial hub showing balances, quick actions, recent transactions, and promotional content. Layout is a single scrollable column with a horizontal card carousel embedded mid-screen.

## Layout & Spacing
Top bar holds avatar + CTA pill + icon. Below, a large welcome heading with an inline icon sits above a segmented action row. The balance section uses a horizontal scroll carousel breaking the vertical rhythm intentionally. Transactions section resumes vertical flow with a section header + "See all" link pattern. A promotional feature card closes the visible scroll area. Generous vertical breathing room between sections (~24–32px). Card carousel bleeds off-screen right to signal scrollability.

## Components
- **Avatar with notification dot** — initials-based circle with a small red presence/alert dot overlaid at bottom-right
- **Referral pill CTA** — green rounded pill with high contrast white text, sits inline in the header row as a persistent but non-intrusive nudge
- **Balance hide toggle** — eye/mask icon button, circular, muted — privacy utility without visual weight
- **Quick action pills** — "Send" filled green, "Add money" and "Request" outlined/ghost style; same pill shape creates visual grouping, filled state signals primary action
- **Currency balance card** — rounded rect card, light gray fill, flag emoji as currency identifier, masked account number, large bold balance figure; dashed-border sibling card for "Add new" currency
- **Pagination dots** — top right corner of screen (outside phone frame) indicating onboarding or carousel state
- **Transaction row** — icon + description + date metadata left, amount + running balance right; "+" amount in green for positive reinforcement
- **Promotional feature card** — dark maroon/deep red background with organic illustrated element, dismissible via ✕ button

## Typography & Color
- **Hierarchy**: Large bold welcome heading (heaviest weight) → section labels (bold, ~18px) → body/metadata (regular, muted gray, smaller)
- **Balance figure**: Extra-large, heavy weight — the number is the hero
- **Color palette**: White base, light gray card fills, green as primary action/brand color, deep red/maroon for promotional card, muted grays for secondary text
- **Green used functionally**: Primary action button, referral CTA, positive transaction amount — consistent semantic meaning
- **Red dot on avatar**: Notification signal only, minimal footprint

## Patterns
- **Asymmetric carousel bleed** — balance cards scroll horizontally while page remains vertical; right card deliberately crops to communicate "more exists" without explicit instruction
- **Action pill hierarchy via fill state** — one filled (primary), two ghost (secondary) — same shape family communicates equal importance while fill creates clear priority without size difference
- **Inline referral CTA in header** — monetization/growth nudge placed at top but styled as a soft pill rather than a banner, avoiding interruptive feel
- **Privacy toggle as first-class control** — balance masking is immediately accessible without burying in settings, acknowledging real-world shoulder-surfing context
- **Dashed border "Add new" card** — uses visual language of an empty/placeholder state within the carousel to prompt expansion behavior; feels native to the flow rather than a separate CTA
- **Transaction amount + running balance pairing** — shows both delta (+30 SGD) and resulting state (31.41 SGD) in a two-line right-aligned stack — reduces need to mentally calculate
- **Dismissible promo card with ✕** — respects user agency on promotional content, builds trust

## Notes
- Green pill referral in header > banner ads — learn from this for growth nudges
- Fill vs ghost same-shape pill = elegant primary/secondary without size hierarchy
- Dashed border card as "add slot" — reuse for any expandable collection UI
- Balance as typographic hero — let the number breathe, everything else is support
- Positive amounts in green = emotional reinforcement, not just data
- Carousel bleed = implicit affordance, no "swipe" label needed
- Dark rich promo card breaks visual monotony of light UI — contrast earns attention
- Avatar notification dot: tiny, precise, doesn't compete

## Screen
A guided program session screen within a wellness/health app. This is a course-style content viewer showing a structured program with sequential lessons. Sits mid-flow after program enrollment — the user is actively working through a multi-part curriculum. Layout is a vertical scroll of lesson cards beneath a fixed branded header, with a clear top-to-bottom progression model.

## Layout & Spacing
Header zone uses the full-bleed blue with illustrated background, creating a distinct "program identity" band. Below, content shifts to white with generous padding. Session metadata (Part/Session label + session title) anchors the top of the scroll area. Cards are separated by dotted vertical connectors suggesting a sequence/path. The active card expands with a full-width thumbnail, description, and duration — while the completed card is compact/collapsed. A peeking card at the bottom signals more content below.

## Components
- **Completed lesson card**: Compact horizontal layout — thumbnail with green checkmark badge overlay, title, content type icon + label, duration. Rounded rect, light border.
- **Active lesson card**: Full-width expanded card with large hero thumbnail (16:9-ish), floating circular play button bottom-right, title, type label, description text, duration pill. Elevated with subtle shadow.
- **Duration pill**: Small rounded rectangle, outlined style, contains time string — minimal, label-like.
- **Dotted vertical connector**: Three-dot ellipsis between cards visually linking them as a sequence.
- **"Up Next" badge**: Dark rounded pill, bottom-right corner, appears over the peeking next card — contextual nudge.
- **Content type label**: Icon + text pairing (video camera icon + "Video") used consistently across cards.
- **Header**: Gradient blue with subtle illustrated moon/star motifs, back arrow left, overflow menu right, program label above title.

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Small caps spaced label ("GUIDED PROGRAM", "PART 1 • SESSION 1") → Large serif/bold session title → Card title in bold sans → Body description in regular weight → Duration in small regular.
- **Color palette**: Vivid royal blue (primary/brand), sky blue (secondary/illustration), white (content surface), dark navy for text, green for completion state, yellow accent (moon emoji/illustration).
- **Color as function**: Blue = program identity and active state. Green = completion. White = neutral content zone. The shift from blue header to white body creates a clear chrome-vs-content separation.

## Patterns
- **Progressive disclosure via card expansion**: Completed items collapse to minimal rows; the current/active item expands fully with description and hero image. Reduces cognitive load while keeping context visible.
- **Dotted path connector**: Borrows from roadmap/journey metaphors — reinforces sequential progression without a traditional numbered list. Subtle but meaningful.
- **Peek pattern for next item**: The bottom card is intentionally cropped with an "Up Next" label — communicates continuity and encourages scroll without explicit CTA button.
- **Completion badge on thumbnail**: Green checkmark overlaid directly on the content thumbnail rather than a separate indicator — keeps the card compact while communicating state clearly.
- **Illustrated header as program identity**: The header isn't just navigation — it carries thematic illustration (moons, stars, night sky) that emotionally frames the content domain. The program "lives" in this header zone.
- **Floating play button**: Positioned bottom-right of the active card thumbnail, outside the natural grid — draws the eye and signals primary action without a separate button row.
- **Dual thumbnail treatment**: Completed card uses a portrait photo thumbnail; active card uses a graphic/illustrated thumbnail with question mark motif — differentiating content types visually.

## Notes
- Collapsed → expanded card states = great pattern for sequential content players
- Dotted connector line between cards > numbered lists for "journey" feel
- Peek + "Up Next" pill = passive scroll encouragement, no pushy CTA
- Header illustration does emotional heavy lifting — don't underestimate theming in wellness
- Duration pill as outlined chip keeps it scannable without visual weight
- Checkmark badge on thumbnail corner = efficient state communication in compact cards
- Play button floating off card edge = breaks grid intentionally to signal interactivity
- White body / colored header split = strong chrome separation pattern

## Screen
Shopping/commerce aggregator home screen — a "shop" tab within what appears to be a financial or payments app. This is the entry point to a shopping discovery experience embedded within a broader app. Layout flows top-to-bottom: full-bleed hero carousel → search bar → featured stores grid. Primary interactive elements: hero carousel, CTA button, search with camera input, store cards, bottom nav.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Hero takes roughly 60% of the visible screen height — aggressive but intentional, treating the carousel like editorial content. Below the fold, content switches to a clean white card-style container with generous internal padding. The transition from dark immersive hero to white content section creates a strong visual break. Store grid uses equal-width 3-column layout with comfortable card padding. Section header uses a classic left-label / right-link split.

---

## Components
- **Hero carousel card** — full-bleed photography with bottom gradient scrim, floating brand logo (white circle, center-positioned), small "Promoted" label above headline, pill CTA button with frosted/translucent dark treatment
- **Carousel dots** — minimal, centered below hero image
- **User avatar** — small circular avatar with letter initial, left of screen title, acts as account access
- **Action icons (top right)** — two circular frosted/semi-transparent icon buttons (wishlist + location/deals)
- **Search bar** — full-width pill shape, light gray fill, camera icon on right for visual search
- **Store cards** — rounded rectangle, white background, brand logo centered, store name below, optional metadata line (cashback in green, "Promoted" in gray)
- **Bottom nav** — 3 icons, minimal, no labels

---

## Typography & Color
- **Hero headline**: Large, bold, white, centered — high contrast over scrim. Two-line layout feels intentional and editorial
- **"Promoted" label**: Small, light weight, white — deliberately understated
- **Section header "Featured stores"**: Bold, dark, medium size
- **"View all"**: Purple/violet accent — consistent action color
- **Cashback text**: Green — functionally signals reward/value, distinct from all other text
- **"Promoted" store label**: Gray — neutral, doesn't compete with cashback green
- Color palette: Dark hero photography → pure white content area. Accent colors: purple (actions), green (value/rewards), gray (secondary info)

---

## Patterns
- **Embedded commerce inside non-commerce app** — shopping as a feature tab, not the whole app. The avatar + "Shop" title mimics a social feed header, making commerce feel native rather than transactional
- **Promoted content with transparency** — "Promoted" label appears both on the hero ad and on store cards, but styled so subtly it doesn't disrupt browsing. Honest without being intrusive
- **Cashback as primary value differentiator** — green cashback percentage is the only colored text in the store cards, immediately drawing the eye. Reward signal is the hook
- **Camera search in the search bar** — visual search affordance baked into the default search field rather than hidden in a separate flow. Suggests "see something, buy it" behavior
- **Floating brand logo on hero** — placing the advertiser's logo as a large white circle mid-image rather than a banner overlay feels more like a collab/editorial treatment than an ad
- **Frosted pill CTA on hero** — semi-transparent dark button rather than a solid color CTA keeps the hero feeling immersive while still providing clear action
- **Gradient scrim for text legibility** — bottom-heavy dark gradient allows white text to sit on photography without a separate text container

---

## Notes
- Cashback in green = instant value signal, steal this for any rewards context
- Camera icon in search bar = don't hide visual search, surface it inline
- Avatar as nav entry point in header — saves a bottom nav slot
- "Promoted" styled as metadata not disclaimer — reduces friction, maintains trust
- Hero logo as floating white circle = feels editorial, not ad-like
- Frosted/translucent pill buttons work well over photography — avoid solid color CTAs on busy images
- Hard break between dark hero and white content = strong section rhythm
- 3-col store grid with logo + name + single metadata line = maximum density without clutter

## Screen
A shopping/commerce aggregator home screen — the discovery and browsing entry point. Sits at the top of the app hierarchy. Layout stacks vertically: full-bleed hero carousel at top, utility search bar in the middle, then a horizontal store grid below. Primary interactive elements: hero carousel with CTA button, search bar with camera icon, store tiles, wishlist and rewards icons in the header.

## Layout & Spacing
Hero takes roughly 55% of the visible screen height — dominant and immersive. Below it, the search bar sits in a clean white section with generous padding, acting as a visual "reset" after the dark hero. The featured stores section uses a 3-column grid with equal-width cards. The transition from dark hero to white content area creates a strong zone separation without any explicit divider line.

## Components
- **Hero carousel card**: Full-bleed image with bottom gradient overlay, large centered headline, small "Promoted" label above headline, floating brand logo medallion (white circle), pill-shaped CTA button in semi-transparent dark style, dot pagination indicators at bottom
- **Avatar + title header**: Small circular avatar with initial letter sits inline with the screen title — personalizes the header without taking space
- **Search bar**: Rounded rectangle, light gray fill, placeholder text left-aligned, camera/visual search icon right-aligned
- **Store tiles**: Rounded square cards with white background, circular brand logo centered, store name below, colored metadata line (cashback in green, "Promoted" in gray)
- **Icon buttons in header**: Circular frosted/gray background, heart and location-pin-style icons

## Typography & Color
Strong two-tier type hierarchy in the hero: small all-caps "Promoted" label in light weight, then a very large bold serif-adjacent headline. Below the hero, type shifts to standard sans-serif. Section headers ("Featured stores") use bold weight; "View all" uses a purple/violet accent color as a functional link signal. Cashback text uses green to signal positive financial value. Overall palette: dark moody hero (near-black gradient), then crisp white for the content zone. Purple is used sparingly as the single accent/action color.

## Patterns
- **Floating brand medallion on hero**: Rather than embedding the brand logo into the image, a white circular badge floats over the product photo — keeps brand attribution clean and legible regardless of background complexity
- **"Promoted" micro-label above headline**: Disclosure placed above the headline so it's read first, not buried — unusually transparent placement
- **Camera icon inside search bar**: Visual search as a first-class feature, not hidden in a menu — signals the app's capability immediately on the home screen
- **Cashback as a store differentiator**: Green cashback percentage shown directly on the store tile, making financial benefit scannable at the browse stage rather than revealed at checkout
- **Mixed trust signals in store grid**: Cashback (user benefit), "Promoted" (paid placement) — both shown honestly at the same level, letting users make informed choices
- **Dot pagination outside the hero card**: Pagination indicators sit at the bottom edge of the hero image, keeping the card itself clean while still communicating carousel depth

## Notes
- Hero-to-white zone break = no divider needed, just color contrast does the work
- Floating logo medallion pattern reusable for any sponsored content card
- Green = earn money, purple = action/navigation — two-color functional system
- Camera in search bar = don't hide power features, surface them on home
- Inline avatar in nav title = cheap personalization, high perceived warmth
- Cashback on browse tile = move value prop earlier in funnel
- "Promoted" above headline = ethical ad labeling pattern worth copying

## Screen
Ride-hailing / multi-service app home screen. Entry point of the main flow — the first screen a logged-in user sees. Layout is a vertical stack: segmented service switcher at top, prominent search bar, recent locations list, horizontal service category grid, and a promotional banner carousel at bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in clearly delineated horizontal bands with generous white space between sections. The search bar sits high and wide — commanding visual priority. Recent locations sit directly below as immediate affordances, reducing friction. The suggestions grid breaks the vertical rhythm with a horizontal scroll. The promo banner bleeds slightly off the right edge, signaling carousel behavior. Section header ("Suggestions") uses a left-label + right "See All" pattern to frame the grid without adding a full row of chrome.

## Components
- **Service tab switcher**: Two tabs with emoji-style icons + text labels; active tab underlined with a bold black bar, inactive tab has no background fill — minimal but clear
- **Search bar**: Pill-shaped, full-width, light gray fill; search icon left-anchored; "Now" time-selector embedded as a secondary pill inside the bar on the right — clever compound input
- **Recent location rows**: Icon (clock) + primary street address + secondary city/state; two-line list items with subtle divider; no chevron, implying tap-to-select
- **Service category cards**: Square cards with rounded corners, white fill, drop shadow; 3D emoji-style illustration centered; label below in small regular weight text; "Promo" badge overlaid top-center in green pill
- **Promo banner**: Full-bleed card with dark maroon background, white bold headline, inline CTA with arrow; decorative illustrated graphic bleeds off right edge; partially visible second card hints at swipe
- **Bottom nav**: Standard 4-item tab bar; active state uses filled icon + bold label; account icon has a blue notification dot

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Bold black for section headers and banner headline; medium weight for tab labels and card labels; regular/secondary gray for sublabels (city, state)
- **Color palette**: Near-white background (#F5F5F5 range), pure black for primary text and active states, mid-gray for secondary text and inactive icons
- **Functional color use**: Green used exclusively for the "Promo" badge — draws eye without competing; maroon banner creates strong contrast zone; blue notification dot on account tab uses color as urgency signal without text

## Patterns
- **Compound input control**: The "Now" time-picker is embedded inside the search bar as a pill — avoids a separate field while keeping scheduling accessible. Elegant space-saving.
- **Predictive recents as primary content**: Recent locations surface immediately below search without requiring any tap — the home screen doubles as a quick-launch pad for repeat trips
- **Badge-as-merchandising**: The "Promo" pill badge on a service card is used to drive discovery of a specific category rather than just flagging new content — promotional logic applied to navigation
- **Partial card bleed**: The promo banner intentionally crops at the right edge to communicate swipeability without any explicit indicator (no dots, no arrows)
- **Emoji/3D illustration in nav**: Using expressive 3D-style icons in the top tab switcher creates warmth and brand personality at the highest-level navigation — unusual for a utility app
- **Cross-service upsell via banner**: The food delivery promo lives on the rides home tab — cross-selling between services without requiring a tab switch, keeping the user in flow

## Notes
- Compound pill-within-search-bar = great pattern for secondary filters that aren't always needed
- Recents as "instant actions" below search — no section header, just implicit utility
- Promo badge on nav card = merchandising at the wayfinding layer
- 3D illustrative icons in tabs = personality without sacrificing clarity
- Bleed crop = cheapest possible carousel affordance
- Dark rich banner color creates visual anchor at bottom of content, draws eye down
- Notification dot on account = unobtrusive but persistent urgency
- "See All" right-aligned to section header = standard but clean; keeps grid tight

## Screen
Profile selection screen for a streaming/entertainment app. This sits at the app entry point — post-login, pre-content. Full-bleed hero image dominates the upper ~65% of the screen, with profile picker anchored to the bottom. Classic "who's watching" gate before accessing the main content library.

## Layout & Spacing
Two distinct zones: an immersive full-bleed content image (top) and a dark rounded-top panel (bottom) that slides up over it. The panel uses a soft dark scrim/surface that creates a natural shelf. Profiles sit in a single horizontal row centered on screen. Add/Edit utility buttons are on a second row, slightly smaller and visually subordinate. Generous vertical spacing between "Choose Your Profile" label and the avatar row.

## Components
- **Hero image**: Full-bleed, edge-to-edge, no overlay or gradient fade — raw content art used as backdrop
- **Bottom sheet panel**: Dark charcoal surface with rounded top corners, floats over the image creating depth
- **Profile avatars**: Large square tiles (~80px equivalent) with rounded corners; each uses a distinct visual style (pixel art, illustrated emoji face, branded kids tile) — no uniformity enforced
- **Avatar labels**: Short name text directly below each tile, white, small weight
- **Utility buttons (Add/Edit)**: Smaller square tiles, same rounded corner radius, dark gray fill with lighter icon — clearly secondary hierarchy
- **"Choose Your Profile" label**: Centered, light gray, medium weight — acts as section header

## Typography & Color
- Single type size for profile names; small, white, regular weight
- "Choose Your Profile" in slightly larger, muted gray — hierarchy through color not size
- Background panel: near-black (~#1a1a1a) with slight transparency hinting at image beneath
- Utility buttons: mid-gray fill (#333–#444 range) keeping them recessive

## Patterns
- **Content-as-wallpaper**: Using actual show artwork as the profile screen background creates immediate content anticipation and brand immersion before any navigation happens
- **Non-uniform avatar system**: Profiles use completely different visual styles (pixel art, emoji, branded tile) — the system accommodates heterogeneous avatar types rather than enforcing a consistent avatar shape/style, which feels personal and lived-in
- **Floating bottom sheet as profile dock**: Rather than a full modal or separate screen, the profile UI anchors to the bottom, keeping the content visible and contextually exciting
- **Utility actions demoted by size**: Add/Edit buttons are the same shape as avatars but noticeably smaller — communicates "these are management tools, not profiles" without labels alone doing the work
- **No back button / nav chrome**: The screen is intentionally stripped of navigation — the only actions are profile selection or management, reducing cognitive load at a high-stakes decision point

## Notes
- Hero art bleeds fully — no safe zone padding, no gradient. Bold choice that trusts the content
- Bottom sheet doesn't fully cover image — intentional reveal of art behind creates layered depth
- Avatar heterogeneity = personality. Don't force avatar uniformity; let users own their tile
- Add/Edit as icon-only buttons with labels below matches avatar pattern — consistent grammar
- Consider: selected/active profile state (scale up? glow ring?) — not shown here but implied need
- Outer presentation frame (light gray card with lock/pause icons) suggests this is a demo/prototype mockup wrapper — smart way to present screens with context controls

## Screen
Profile selection screen for a streaming/entertainment app. This sits at the entry point of the app flow — appearing after launch or when switching accounts. Full-bleed hero artwork dominates the upper ~70% of the screen, with profile picker anchored to the bottom. Layout is vertically split: immersive media content above, functional UI below.

## Layout & Spacing
The screen uses a hard visual split — cinematic artwork fills the top portion edge-to-edge with no padding, while a dark rounded panel rises from the bottom containing all interactive elements. Profile avatars sit in a single horizontal row, evenly spaced. Below them, "Add" and "Edit" utility buttons are centered as a secondary row. The bottom panel uses generous vertical padding, giving the UI room to breathe against the dark background. The artwork bleeds behind the bottom panel slightly, creating depth.

## Components
- **Hero artwork panel** — Full-bleed, no border radius clipping at top, fades naturally into the dark bottom section
- **Profile avatars** — Large square tiles (~72–80pt) with rounded corners, styled as app icons rather than circular avatars; each has a distinct visual identity (pixel art, illustrated emoji face, branded kids tile)
- **Profile labels** — Small white text centered below each avatar
- **Add/Edit buttons** — Rounded rectangle ghost buttons with icon + label, dark gray fill, slightly muted to signal secondary priority
- **"Choose Your Profile" label** — Small, light-weight text centered above the avatar row, acting as a section header
- **Outer UI chrome** — Lock and pause icons in circular pill buttons float outside the phone frame (tutorial/demo overlay context)

## Typography & Color
Type hierarchy is minimal: section label ("Choose Your Profile") is small, regular weight, white. Profile names are slightly smaller, white, regular weight. Button labels ("Add," "Edit") match profile name size. No large display type in the functional UI — the artwork's title treatment handles visual drama. Color palette: deep black/dark charcoal for the bottom panel, white text throughout, avatar tiles introduce the only color variety (warm yellow, pixel art earth tones, vibrant kids branding). The red/dark anime artwork provides the emotional color backdrop.

## Patterns
- **Content-as-wallpaper profile selection** — Rather than a neutral or branded background, currently trending/featured content serves as the backdrop, reinforcing the value proposition before the user even selects a profile. Clever retention mechanic disguised as UI chrome.
- **App-icon-style avatars** — Using square rounded-rect avatar tiles instead of circles differentiates this from social apps and reinforces the "this is your personal app experience" metaphor. Each avatar has a completely unique visual style, signaling personality customization.
- **Floating bottom sheet without a sheet** — The dark bottom section isn't a modal sheet; it's baked into the layout, but achieves the same visual grounding effect. The slight overlap with the artwork creates layering without a hard edge.
- **Kids profile visual differentiation** — The kids profile tile uses distinctly branded, colorful design that immediately reads as "different/restricted" — communicating parental control context without any explanatory text.
- **Utility actions deprioritized by form** — Add/Edit are given identical visual weight to each other but clearly subordinate to profile selection through size reduction and ghost button treatment. No confusion about primary vs. secondary actions.
- **No navigation chrome during profile selection** — The screen strips away all nav elements, keeping focus entirely on the selection task. Clean entry ritual.

## Notes
- Content-as-background = free marketing + emotional priming before any interaction
- Square avatar tiles feel more "owned" than circles — consider for multi-profile apps
- Kids profile should always look visually distinct at a glance — color/branding does the job text can't
- Bottom anchor panel with soft overlap into hero = elegant split without hard dividers
- Ghost icon buttons for utility actions (add/edit) — keeps them accessible but visually quiet
- Profile selection as a "moment" — full screen, no distractions, almost ceremonial UX
- Outer floating controls (lock/pause) suggest this UI pattern works well in kiosk/demo modes too

## Screen
Social media feed home screen. Photo/content sharing app. Entry point of the main experience — the first screen users land on after login. Layout is vertically stacked: top nav bar → horizontal stories tray → feed post header → full-bleed post image → bottom tab bar.

## Layout & Spacing
- Top bar uses three-zone layout: action left, branded title center, action right
- Stories tray is a horizontally scrolling row with consistent avatar sizing and label beneath each
- Feed post header is a tight single row: avatar + username string + follow CTA + overflow menu
- Post image is edge-to-edge (full bleed), no horizontal margin — creates immersive content experience
- Generous vertical rhythm between stories and feed post header; post header sits close to image

## Components
- **Story avatars**: Circular, bordered with gradient ring (pink-to-orange-to-yellow) indicating unseen content; "Your story" uses a plain photo with a blue "+" badge overlay
- **Story labels**: Small, truncated text beneath each circle, centered alignment
- **Post header row**: Small circular avatar (no gradient ring — already-seen or non-story account), username in medium weight, inline "and [collaborator]" secondary attribution, pill-shaped "Follow" button in light gray, "···" overflow icon
- **Carousel position indicator**: Small pill badge top-right of image ("2/5") — semi-transparent dark background, white text
- **Bottom nav**: Icon-only, 5 tabs, monochrome icons, active state shown via filled vs. outline icon treatment
- **Top nav heart icon**: Has a small notification dot (red) indicating pending activity

## Typography & Color
- Title uses a script/display typeface — brand identity, not UI font
- Username text: medium weight sans-serif, dark on white
- "and wrongmarfa" secondary attribution: same weight, slightly subordinate visually via positioning not size
- Story labels: small, regular weight, gray
- Palette: white background, near-black text, gradient accents (pink/purple/orange) for story rings, red notification dot, light gray pill button — color used functionally to signal state (unseen story = gradient, notification = red dot)

## Patterns
- **Collaborative attribution inline**: "username and collaborator" in a single line within the post header — elegant way to surface co-authorship without adding a second row or disrupting layout density
- **Carousel progress as badge, not dots**: Using "2/5" text pill instead of dot indicators saves horizontal space and communicates total count at a glance — especially useful for longer carousels where dot rows would overflow
- **Stories tray as social proof + navigation hybrid**: The tray serves dual purpose — content discovery and social presence signaling (whose story is active)
- **Notification dot on heart icon**: Rather than a badge number, a simple dot keeps the top bar visually clean while still communicating pending activity
- **Follow CTA in feed header**: Contextual follow button placed inline with post attribution — reduces friction by surfacing action exactly where interest is sparked (mid-scroll, at point of content exposure)
- **Full-bleed image with no card container**: Post image breaks to screen edges, no rounded corners or padding — maximizes content area and creates editorial feel

## Notes
- Inline co-author attribution = elegant, worth stealing for collab features
- "2/5" pill > dot indicators for carousels with 4+ items
- Notification dot only (no count) = cleaner top bar
- Follow CTA inline with username = right-time-right-place conversion pattern
- Full bleed image = no card = more immersive, feels editorial not app-like
- Story ring gradient = strong unseen/seen state differentiation without text
- Script wordmark in nav = brand presence without logo image asset

## Screen
Portfolio dashboard in a retail investing/brokerage app. This is the home/landing screen after login — the primary financial overview. Layout flows top-to-bottom: nav bar → large portfolio value → segmented data tabs → performance chart → time range selector → buying power card → onboarding nudge card → bottom tab nav.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is left-aligned with generous top padding before the portfolio value, creating visual breathing room. The chart takes up roughly 40% of the screen height — intentionally dominant. Below the chart, cards are inset with subtle rounded corners and light shadow, creating a layered feel. The onboarding card peeks from the bottom, implying scrollable content below. Horizontal rhythm is consistent with ~20px side margins throughout.

## Components
- **Portfolio value display** — oversized bold numeral, no label, context implied by tab selection
- **Segmented tab bar** — text-only, underline indicator on active tab (blue), inactive tabs in muted blue/gray; no pill/background treatment
- **Performance indicator row** — red badge icon + colored text showing delta value and percentage, inline info icon
- **Line chart** — minimal, no grid lines, single dashed horizontal reference line (baseline), small circle endpoint marker; negative performance rendered in red/pink
- **Time range selector** — text-based pill for active state (outlined box, pink tint), plain text for inactive; gear icon at end for customization
- **Buying power card** — white rounded card, label in blue, value in black, pill-shaped "Deposit" CTA right-aligned
- **Onboarding nudge card** — partially visible, badge counter (blue circle "7"), bell icon in soft blue circle, section label in blue, descriptive body copy
- **FAB-style icon button** — lime green circle in nav area with sparkle icon; high contrast, draws immediate attention
- **Rewards pill button** — outlined, arrow indicator, top-right of value area

## Typography & Color
**Hierarchy:** Portfolio value (~36px bold) → section labels (14px medium blue) → body/values (16px regular black) → metadata (13px muted)

**Color palette:**
- Primary blue (~#4B5FFF) — active states, labels, interactive elements
- Lime/chartreuse green — single FAB button, high-contrast accent
- Red/pink (~#E8365D) — negative performance, chart line, active time range pill tint
- Near-white background with white cards
- Muted gray for inactive tab text and icons

Color is used functionally: red = loss/negative, blue = interactive/informational, green = action/AI feature.

## Patterns
- **Dominant metric with no label** — the $46.00 stands alone without "Portfolio Value" label; the tab below contextualizes it, reducing visual clutter while trusting user context
- **Reference line without axes** — the dashed horizontal baseline on the chart communicates "starting point" without adding full axis labels, keeping the chart emotionally readable rather than analytically dense
- **Peek card as scroll invitation** — the partially visible onboarding card with a bold badge number creates curiosity and implies depth without requiring an explicit "scroll for more" prompt
- **Contextual CTA placement** — "Deposit" lives inside the buying power card rather than as a standalone button, tying the action directly to the metric it affects
- **Accent color isolation** — lime green appears exactly once (the AI/sparkle button), making it impossible to miss and signaling a distinct feature tier
- **Time range gear icon** — adding a settings affordance to the time range row suggests customizability without cluttering the UI with options upfront
- **Badge on partially hidden card** — the "7" badge on the peeking card adds urgency to something not yet fully visible, a clever pull mechanic

## Notes
- Lone oversized number + tabs below = elegant label-free hierarchy trick
- Dashed baseline > full axes for emotional chart reading
- Peek + badge = scroll bait that actually works
- One lime accent = instant hierarchy without a second brand color
- Deposit CTA inside metric card = contextual action design
- Gear in time selector = progressive disclosure for power users
- Consider: red tint on active time range pill mirrors chart color — subtle state reinforcement
- Info ⓘ icon next to volatile metric = trust-building micro-pattern

## Screen
Portfolio dashboard in a retail investing/trading app. This is the home screen after login — the primary financial overview state. Layout flows top-to-bottom: nav bar → portfolio value → tab switcher → performance delta → line chart → time range selector → buying power card → onboarding nudge card → bottom nav.

---

## Layout & Spacing

Content is left-aligned with generous top padding around the portfolio value, giving it breathing room as the hero element. The chart occupies roughly 40% of the visible screen height — dominant but not crowded. Cards below the chart use rounded rectangles with subtle shadows, creating a layered shelf feel. The time range row sits flush between chart and cards, acting as a visual separator. Horizontal margins are consistent (~20px) throughout.

---

## Components

- **Portfolio value (hero number):** Extra-large bold sans-serif, left-aligned, no label — the number speaks for itself
- **Rewards pill button:** Outlined rounded pill, right-aligned next to value — low-weight secondary action
- **Tab switcher:** Underline-style tabs (not pills), active state uses a bold underline in blue/indigo; inactive tabs in muted blue-gray
- **Performance delta badge:** Small red circle icon + colored text inline — negative state rendered in red/pink with a downward arrow icon
- **Line chart:** Minimal, no fill/gradient — just a crisp pink/red stroke with a single endpoint dot marker; dashed horizontal reference line at opening value
- **Time range selector:** Text-only options with the active state (1D) in a soft pink outlined box — matches chart color for semantic consistency
- **Gear icon:** Sits at the end of the time range row — settings access without breaking the row rhythm
- **Buying power card:** Rounded card with label + value left, ghost/outlined "Deposit" button right — clear CTA without visual aggression
- **Onboarding nudge card:** Partially peeking card with a bold blue notification badge (7) — uses peek pattern to signal more content below
- **Nav bar active icon:** Filled blue square icon for active state vs. outline icons for inactive

---

## Typography & Color

**Type hierarchy:**
- Hero: ~36px bold black (portfolio value)
- Section labels: ~13px medium, muted blue-gray (tab items, card labels)
- Body: ~15px regular black (card values, notification copy)
- Micro: ~12px for delta percentage and "today" label

**Color palette:**
- White background, near-white card surfaces
- Electric blue/indigo: active tab underline, active nav icon, card accent text, notification badge
- Red/pink: negative performance — used consistently across delta text, chart stroke, and active time range pill (semantic color threading)
- Lime/chartreuse: floating action button in nav header — high contrast accent, draws eye immediately
- Gray: inactive nav icons, muted tab labels

---

## Patterns

**Semantic color threading:** The loss state color (red/pink) is applied across three separate elements — the delta indicator, the chart line, and the active time range selector — creating a coherent visual language that communicates "this is the current negative period" without any extra labels.

**Dashed reference line:** A horizontal dashed line at the start-of-day value gives the chart a zero-reference without cluttering the visualization with a full grid.

**Peek card pattern:** The onboarding/notification card is intentionally cut off at the bottom, signaling scrollability and creating curiosity — the badge count (7) amplifies urgency without a modal interrupt.

**Tab-as-view-switcher over segmented control:** Using underline tabs instead of a segmented pill control keeps the header area light and allows more tab options (4) without crowding.

**Floating accent FAB in header:** The lime green circular button in the top nav breaks the monochrome header in a deliberate, brand-forward way — not a traditional FAB position but used as a primary discovery/feature entry point.

**Buying power as inline card:** Rather than burying buying power in account details, it's surfaced as a card with a direct deposit CTA — reducing friction for the most common next action after checking portfolio performance.

---

## Notes

- Red chart + red time selector pill = elegant state-aware design, steal this
- No chart fill/gradient = cleaner, more precise feel than typical finance apps
- Dashed baseline reference line > full grid for minimal charts
- Peek card + badge count = passive notification without interruption
- Lime FAB in header = brand moment, breaks visual monotony without chaos
- Outlined "Deposit" button = secondary but present — doesn't compete with chart
- Tab switcher with 4 options works because labels are short; would break at 5+
- Portfolio value has no label — confident, assumes context from screen

## Screen
Mental wellness / mood tracking app. This is the home/check-in screen — the primary daily entry point. Layout is vertically stacked: sparse nav header, large editorial headline, dominant circular mood selector, stat pills, and tab bar. The circular element is the hero interaction.

## Layout & Spacing
Near-full-bleed dark canvas with generous vertical breathing room between headline and circle. The circle occupies roughly 50% of the visible content area — intentionally oversized to signal importance. Stat pills sit in a tight band just above the tab bar, creating a clear "summary zone" separated from the interaction zone. Horizontal padding is consistent and tight, letting the circle feel expansive.

## Components
- **Gradient mood ring**: Large circular track with a full-spectrum gradient (gold → orange → red → purple → blue → teal → green → yellow). A gap at the top-left suggests a draggable or tappable selector. The active position is marked by a bright yellow-gold terminus with a soft glow/blur effect.
- **Center CTA button**: Small white circular button with a checkmark inside, labeled "Check in" below in muted white. Minimal, pill-adjacent feel.
- **Stat pills**: Two dark rounded rectangles with a large muted numeral on the left and a two-line label on the right. Low contrast, secondary hierarchy.
- **Tab bar icons**: Custom line-art icons, slightly unconventional (diagonal arrow for Tools, stylized figure pair for Friends).
- **Header icons**: Hexagonal settings icon (left), share/export icon (right) — both minimal stroke style.

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: Large serif (likely a display serif), bold weight, white on black — warm and editorial, unusual for wellness apps which typically use sans-serif
- **Stat numerals**: Large, light-weight sans-serif in medium gray — deliberately de-emphasized
- **Labels**: Small, regular-weight sans-serif in muted gray
- **Color palette**: Near-black background (#0a0a0a range), full rainbow gradient on the ring, white for primary text and CTA icon, muted grays for secondary content
- Color is used functionally on the ring to encode emotional spectrum — the position on the ring maps to a feeling/mood

## Patterns
- **Spectrum-as-input**: Using a full-color gradient ring as a mood selector is a non-verbal, emotionally resonant input method — avoids clinical labels until selection is made. Color does the semantic work.
- **Serif headline on dark**: Deliberately warm and conversational — the serif choice humanizes what could feel like a data-entry screen. Creates a journal-like intimacy.
- **Time-aware copy**: "This morning?" suggests the app personalizes the prompt by time of day — micro-personalization that increases perceived attentiveness.
- **Glow terminus**: The active point on the ring has a soft luminous blur, giving the static screenshot a sense of motion and interactivity — implies drag/rotate gesture affordance without explicit instruction.
- **Understated CTA**: The check-in button is intentionally small and centered inside the ring — it doesn't compete with the mood selection gesture. The ring IS the primary interaction; the button confirms.
- **Stat pills as passive feedback**: Showing "5 unique feelings" and "2 day streak" at the bottom reframes data as encouragement without making it the focus. Gamification hidden in plain sight.
- **Negative space as calm**: The large empty black area inside the ring and above the stats creates visual stillness — appropriate for the emotional context of the app.

## Notes
- Serif headline = warmth hack for clinical/data contexts
- Gradient ring as emotional spectrum — no words needed until after selection
- Glow effect on interactive terminus = motion implied in static UI
- Tiny centered confirm button inside hero element = hierarchy through scale inversion
- Time-of-day personalization in prompt copy = cheap but effective intimacy signal
- Streak + unique count in muted pills = soft gamification, not aggressive
- Full black bg + rainbow ring = high contrast drama without feeling aggressive
- Consider: ring gap placement as "neutral" anchor point

## Screen
Food delivery app — individual menu item detail/customization screen. Sits after tapping a menu item from a restaurant listing. Full-screen modal or sheet layered over the restaurant page. Layout: hero food image top half, scrollable content bottom half with item name, price, description, and add-on options.

## Layout & Spacing
Content split roughly 45/55 between image and detail area. White card sheet slides up over the restaurant background, creating clear depth layering. Item name and price sit on the same baseline row, price right-aligned. Description text is muted and sits in a comfortable breathing zone before the add-ons section. Add-ons list uses generous row height (~60px) for easy touch targets. Thin dividers between each add-on row maintain visual separation without heaviness.

## Components
- **Hero image**: Full-bleed, edge-to-edge within the modal, no border radius clipping — food photography bleeds to edges
- **Close (X) + Share buttons**: Floating icon buttons with subtle semi-transparent or white circular backgrounds, overlaid on image corners
- **Restaurant info card**: Partially visible behind/above the modal — creates a layered peek effect showing context
- **Price display**: Large, bold numeral right-aligned next to item name; "Base price" label in small gray below — two-line price block
- **Add-ons checkboxes**: Rounded square checkboxes, unselected state shown in light gray outline; label left, price delta right-aligned with "+" prefix
- **Section header row**: "Add-Ons" bold left, "Optional, max 4" constraint label right in muted small text

## Typography & Color
- **Hierarchy**: Item name ~22px bold black → price ~22px bold black → description ~14px gray → add-on labels ~16px medium → price deltas ~16px regular
- **Color palette**: White sheet background, near-black for primary text, medium gray for secondary/description text, gold/amber star for rating, green accent implied by brand header

- **Constraint labeling inline with section header**: "Optional, max 4" placed right-aligned on the same row as the section title — communicates rules at the moment of decision, not as a separate instruction block
- **Base price labeling**: Explicitly labeling the price as "Base price" sets expectation that the number will change — primes users for add-on pricing before they interact
- **Floating action buttons on image**: X and share sit on the image rather than in a nav bar — preserves full image real estate while keeping controls accessible
- **Truncated scroll hint**: Bottom add-on item cuts off slightly, signaling more content below — classic scroll affordance without any explicit indicator

## Notes
- "Base price" label = smart expectation-setting before upsell
- Constraint copy (max 4) right-aligned in header row = rules at point of action
- Partial restaurant card peek = wayfinding without navigation
- Floating X on image > nav bar back button for modal feel
- Cut-off last list item = scroll invitation, no "see more" needed
- Price delta with "+" prefix = clear additive framing, no color needed
- Checkbox rows: wide touch targets, minimal visual noise

- **Rewards floating card** — White pill/card with brand logo badge + label, floats over the bottom of the hero image. Soft shadow implied. Acts as a persistent entry point
- **Bottom nav** — Clean minimal icons, active state underlined (Home), small orange notification dot on Rewards tab

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: All-caps small tracking label → large bold headline → pill button label
- **Palette**: Deep, moody food photography as the "background color"; acid chartreuse for primary CTA; white for all overlaid UI text and icons; warm yellow-gold for brand badge on rewards card
- **Color function**: Chartreuse is used exclusively for the primary action — maximum attention with minimum usage. White handles all secondary UI chrome. The notification dot is the only other accent color (orange/red), used sparingly for urgency

## Patterns
- **No scrim hero** — Trusts the photography to provide natural contrast rather than adding a darkening overlay. Requires careful art direction but feels more premium
- **Right-rail utility clustering** — Profile and QR scanner are stacked vertically on the right edge, keeping the left side clean for editorial content. Treats them as secondary tools, not primary nav
- **Floating rewards card as persistent CTA** — Rather than burying rewards in the nav, a floating card overlaps the hero/nav boundary, giving it visual priority without interrupting the image. The overlap creates depth and draws the eye
- **Notification dot on nav item** — Subtle orange dot on Rewards tab creates urgency/pull without a badge number, implying "something new" without being aggressive
- **Collaboration framing** — Eyebrow text uses "X" collab format as editorial shorthand, borrowed from streetwear/fashion culture — signals premium limited-time positioning before the user reads the headline
- **QR code as first-class action** — Placing a QR scanner icon at the top level (not buried in menus) suggests in-store scanning is a core use case, not an afterthought

## Notes
- Acid yellow CTA on dark photo = instant hierarchy, no competition
- Floating card bridging hero → nav is a great pattern for loyalty/rewards surfacing
- Right-side icon rail keeps left editorial zone clean — worth stealing
- No scrim = requires intentional photography brief; design and photo must co-design
- Collab eyebrow label as cultural signal, not just info
- QR at top level = signals omnichannel intent from the home screen
- Notification dot without count = softer urgency, less anxiety-inducing than badge numbers

- **Floating bottom card**: White, heavily rounded corners (~20px), subtle shadow, sits above the map with visible gap from screen edges

- **Flight row**: Large bold duration left-aligned, flight number with airline icon, status badge in green, airport codes with departure/arrival times, green circular airport icons, +1 day indicator

## Typography & Color
Strong hierarchy: duration "5h" is display-sized (~40px), bold, black — immediate scan anchor. "41 MINUTES" is tiny caps/label weight below it. Route name "Jakarta to Seoul" is medium-weight body. - Globe viz as hero = emotional hook, not just utility

- Green time values = status color bleeds into data, smart redundancy
- Floating card gap = map feels real, not covered
- Vertical icon cluster for layer toggles — saves horizontal space
- "+1" superscript for next-day arrival — steal this
- Dropdown on screen title = multi-context switching pattern worth using broadly

Active focus/productivity session screen in a distraction-blocking app. This is the "in-session" state — the user has started a timed focus block and is viewing its status. Full-screen image bleeds edge-to-edge, transitioning into a dark bottom sheet via a natural gradient fade — no hard edge. ## Components

- **Timeline scrubber** — thin horizontal line with tick marks and a white dot indicator showing elapsed time. Start/end times flanking it. Minimal, clock-like.
- **Metadata cards (2-up)** — dark rounded rectangles, slightly lighter than background. Left: "Block List" with emoji icon + sub-metadata (counts with icons). Right: "DIFFICULTY" with label + value. Both have chevrons indicating drilldown.

**Type hierarchy:**

- Timer ("00:04:59"): monospace-style weight inline with smaller label text — creates visual anchor
- Card labels: small caps / uppercase tracking for "DIFFICULTY" label; regular weight for values
- Sub-metadata: small, muted white

**Color palette:**
- Background: near-black (#0a0a0a range)
- Cards: dark gray, slightly elevated
- Primary CTA: lime-green → cyan gradient — soft, optimistic

- **Dual-weight CTA hierarchy without buttons** — "Snooze" gets a full gradient pill; "Leave Early" gets only colored text. No secondary button shape. The asymmetry communicates consequence without needing warning dialogs.

- **Emoji as functional icon** — the 🔴 on "Block List" is doing real UI work: it's a quick-scan color signal, not just decoration.
- **"Edit Session" floated over image** — keeps the photo uncluttered while still surfacing an important action contextually at the boundary between image and data.

- Nature wallpaper as UX tool — not just pretty, it's behavioral design
- Gradient dissolve > hard sheet edge for immersive transitions
- Monospace timer inline with prose label = elegant pairing
- Snooze gradient: green→cyan = "safe exit" color language
- Destructive action as bare text only — no button chrome needed when color does the work
- Timeline with real clock times > abstract % progress — anchors user in reality
- 2-up card grid for session metadata = scannable, drillable, compact
- Floating pill edit button at image/content boundary = clever placement
- Consider: emoji as status/category icon in dark UI — high contrast, zero icon library needed

- **Active filter chip uses border, not fill**: Avoids heavy color blocking on the selected state; the outlined treatment feels lighter and doesn't compete with the photography below.

## Notes
- Mini map PiP inside card photo = genius — spatial info without navigation cost
- Green accent used ONLY on map CTA — color as a single functional signal
- Illustrated chip icons > generic icons for lifestyle/outdoor apps
- Download + bookmark as separate persistent card actions — offline-first thinking surfaced early
- Dot separators for trail stats: compact, no visual noise
- Consider: how does card feel with no photo? Fallback state needed
- Filter chip scroll implies more — consider fade edge on right to signal overflow
- Floating pill CTA on image: works because high contrast color, would fail with busy background

## Notes

- Bookmark ≠ Download — separate these intents explicitly
- Filter chips with illustrated icons >> generic icons for brand feel
- Float the map toggle over the list, don't make it a tab
