## Screen
A screen time / digital wellness dashboard — the home/summary screen of a focus or app-blocking app. Sits at the top of the navigation hierarchy as the primary daily overview. Layout is a single vertical scroll column: hero visual zone → primary metric → secondary stats row → timeline chart → offline summary card → primary CTA → tab bar.

---

## Layout & Spacing

Content is organized in clear horizontal bands with generous vertical breathing room between sections. The 3D gem hero occupies roughly 35% of the screen height — an unusually large allocation for a decorative/status element. Stats below the hero are arranged in a 3-column grid (most used apps / focus score / pickups) with tight internal spacing but clear column separation. The bar chart sits edge-to-edge as a full-width band. The offline card and CTA button are grouped tightly at the bottom, creating a natural action zone. Bottom nav is minimal — 3 items only.

---

## Components

- **3D Hero Object** — A photorealistic opal gem on a pedestal, centered, with a soft teal ambient glow radiating outward against pure black. Functions as a mood/status indicator, not just decoration.
- **Primary Metric Display** — Oversized time value ("1h 38m") in a warm yellow-green, with a small all-caps label beneath in muted gray.
- **3-Column Stats Row** — App icons used as data glyphs under "Most Used." Focus Score and Pickups rendered as plain large numerals with uppercase micro-labels.
- **Bar Chart** — Thin vertical bars, color-coded: teal for normal usage, red/pink for a spike. Unfilled future hours shown as dark ghost bars. Time axis labels in small gray text.
- **Offline Summary Card** — Flat dark card with emoji weather icon, bold label, right-aligned duration value, and a secondary percentage line below.
- **Primary CTA Button** — Full-width pill, gradient fill from lime green to mint/teal, play icon + label. High contrast against the dark background.
- **Tab Bar** — Minimal 3-tab bar, icon + label, active state uses filled/white icon.

---

## Typography & Color

**Type hierarchy:**
- Hero number: ~48–52pt, medium-bold weight, warm yellow-green
- Section labels: ~10pt, all-caps, tracked out, muted gray
- Stat values (Focus Score, Pickups): ~22–24pt, white, medium weight
- Chart axis labels: ~10pt, gray
- Card label: ~18pt, white, semibold
- Card secondary: ~13pt, muted gray

**Color palette:**
- Background: near-pure black (#0a0a0a range)
- Accent/primary: warm yellow-green (lime) for the hero metric and CTA gradient
- Secondary accent: teal/cyan for chart bars and gem glow
- Alert: coral/red-pink for the usage spike bar
- Text: white primary, mid-gray secondary
- CTA: lime-to-mint gradient pill

Color is used functionally — the red bar signals a problematic usage spike, the lime/teal palette signals positive focus metrics, and the glow around the gem echoes the overall health "aura."

---

## Patterns

- **Anthropomorphized status object** — The gem acts as a living avatar of your focus health. Its visual state (glow, clarity) implies it could change based on behavior — a gamification hook without explicit game mechanics. Extremely high emotional engagement for a data screen.
- **Data as ambient art** — Rather than leading with charts, the screen leads with a beautiful object. The data is subordinate to the emotional framing. Unusual and effective for a wellness context.
- **Ghost bars for future time** — Showing the remaining hours of the day as faded bars sets a mental model of the full day, not just past usage. Creates implicit goal-setting pressure.
- **Positive reframe metric** — "Time Offline: 12h 5m / 75% of your day" reframes the data positively (time away from phone) rather than leading with shame about screen time. Behavioral design choice.
- **Warm accent on primary metric** — Using yellow-green (not white) for the main number makes it feel like a living readout, not a static label. Draws the eye immediately after the gem.
- **Emoji as icon in data cards** — The weather emoji next to "Time Offline" adds warmth and personality to what could be a cold stat row. Breaks the clinical feel.
- **CTA placement as commitment device** — "Block Now" is the only action on the screen, placed immediately after seeing your usage data. The emotional sequence (see data → feel something → act) is deliberate.

---

## Notes

- Gem-as-avatar = massive opportunity in wellness/habit apps. State changes (cracked, glowing, dim) could replace progress bars entirely
- Positive reframe stats > shame stats — always show the "good" version of the number
- Ghost future bars = underused pattern in time-based charts
- Lime-to-mint gradient on pill CTA feels fresh against dark UI — steal this
- Weather emoji in data rows = personality without illustration budget
- 3-tab nav is the right call — don't add tabs just to have them
- Full-width hero image zone (~35% screen) is bold; only works if the visual is genuinely stunning
- All-caps micro-labels + large numerals = reliable stat display pattern, works here cleanly

## Screen
Workout logging modal in a fitness/health tracking app. This is a manual entry flow — likely accessed from a "+" action or log screen. The user is adding a past or current workout with activity type, time, duration, and perceived exertion. Sheet-style modal with a close (×) and a primary CTA at the bottom.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct card groups separated by clear gaps: (1) workout metadata rows, (2) effort/feel section with slider, (3) optional fields. Each group uses a rounded card container. The effort section breaks from the row pattern to expand vertically, giving it visual weight proportional to its importance. Generous internal padding throughout. The CTA sits outside the card groupings with breathing room above it.

---

## Components
- **List rows with disclosure chevrons** — label left, value right, subtle separator lines within card. Values are bold/dark, labels are regular weight.
- **"As Usual" smart suggestion pill** — small rounded badge with a sparkle/AI icon prefix. Sits inline with the section label. Lightly bordered, not filled.
- **Effort description block** — bold label ("Moderate") + descriptive subtext in gray. Acts as a real-time tooltip for the slider value.
- **Effort slider** — custom gradient track: blue → yellow → orange → purple, representing exertion spectrum. Hatched/diagonal stripe pattern overlaid on the left portion to indicate "your usual range." White circular thumb. A red pill badge ("ABOVE USUAL RANGE") floats above the thumb contextually.
- **"YOUR USUAL RANGE" label** — small caps, positioned below the hatched zone as a legend annotation.
- **CTA button** — full-width, rounded, warm orange. Two-line label: primary action ("Add") large, secondary context ("With Effort Above Your Usual Range") smaller beneath it.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Hierarchy**: Section labels in regular weight gray → values in semibold dark → section headers ("Moderate") in bold black → descriptive text in light gray.
- **Color palette**: Near-white background, white cards, dark near-black text. Accent orange for CTA and slider warm zone. Gradient slider spans cool blue → warm yellow-orange → muted purple. Red for the "above range" alert badge. Gray for optional/secondary states.
- **Color used functionally**: The slider gradient maps effort intensity visually (cool = easy, warm = hard, purple = extreme). Orange CTA reinforces the "above usual" state — it's not a neutral blue, it's contextually warm/alert-toned.

---

## Patterns
- **Contextual CTA label mutation** — The button text changes to reflect the user's current input state ("With Effort Above Your Usual Range"). The button itself becomes a summary confirmation, not just a generic "Save." Extremely useful for reducing second-guessing.
- **Personalized range visualization on slider** — Rather than a generic slider, the track encodes the user's historical baseline as a hatched zone. This transforms a simple input into a self-referential benchmark tool.
- **Floating contextual badge on slider thumb** — The "ABOVE USUAL RANGE" pill appears dynamically above the thumb only when relevant. Avoids cluttering the UI by default while surfacing meaningful insight at the moment of decision.
- **AI/smart default pill inline with field label** — The "As Usual" suggestion sits right next to the question label, offering a one-tap escape hatch without taking up a separate row. Low-friction default with sparkle icon signaling intelligence.
- **Effort description as plain language translation** — Converts a slider position into a human sentence ("Starting to feel the work, but still comfy enough to talk in shorter spurts."). Bridges the gap between abstract scale and felt experience.
- **Optional fields deprioritized visually** — "Active Energy" and "Distance" use gray "Optional" labels instead of empty states or placeholders, signaling they're non-blocking without hiding them entirely.

---

## Notes
- CTA as dynamic summary = powerful pattern for any multi-input form
- Slider + personal baseline overlay → steal for any metric with historical context (sleep, mood, calories)
- Gradient track encodes meaning, not just decoration
- "As Usual" smart pill = quick escape from friction; works for any repeating behavior log
- Two-line CTA button: line 1 = action, line 2 = consequence/context
- Plain language effort descriptions = accessibility + delight, reduces cognitive load on abstract scales
- Hatched zone as "normal range" indicator reusable in health, finance, performance dashboards

## Screen
Workout logging modal in a fitness/health tracking app. This is a data entry screen appearing mid-flow after a workout is detected or manually initiated. User is confirming workout details before saving. Modal sheet layout with a close button, centered title, grouped form rows, a custom effort slider section, and a full-width CTA at the bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct content groups separated by generous white space: (1) core workout metadata rows, (2) perceived effort section with slider, (3) optional supplemental fields. The effort section breaks the row pattern intentionally — it expands vertically to accommodate the slider visualization, creating a visual anchor in the middle of the form. Consistent horizontal padding across all groups. The CTA floats at the bottom with clear breathing room above it.

## Components
- **Form rows**: Light gray rounded-rectangle containers, label left / value+chevron right. Clean, minimal, no borders — background differentiation only.
- **"As Usual" pill button**: Small outlined capsule with a sparkle/AI icon prefix. Suggests smart defaulting. Subtle, non-intrusive placement inline with section label.
- **Effort slider**: Custom gradient track (blue → yellow → orange → purple) representing intensity spectrum. Hatched/diagonal stripe pattern overlaid on the "your usual range" zone. Red pill badge ("ABOVE USUAL RANGE") floats above the thumb. Thumb is a white circle. Tick marks visible on the right half of the track.
- **CTA button**: Full-width, warm orange rounded rectangle. Two-line label — primary action text large, secondary context text smaller below it.
- **Optional rows**: Same row style but value text is muted gray "Optional" instead of a real value — communicates non-required fields without hiding them.

## Typography & Color
- **Hierarchy**: Modal title medium weight center-aligned → section label regular weight → value bold/semibold right-aligned → descriptive body text light gray below effort label.
- **Effort label "Moderate"**: Bold, larger — acts as a heading for the slider section.
- **Descriptor text**: Small, gray, italic-adjacent — adds qualitative meaning to the quantitative slider position.
- **Color palette**: Neutral white/light gray base. Orange as the primary action color (CTA + "above usual" state). Gradient track uses full warm-to-cool spectrum. Red for the alert badge. Muted gray for optional/secondary text.
- **Color used functionally**: Orange signals elevated effort state and propagates to the CTA — the button color matches the effort level selected, creating semantic continuity.

## Patterns
- **Contextual CTA label**: The button doesn't just say "Add" — it says "Add / With Effort Above Your Usual Range." The secondary line dynamically reflects the user's current selection, confirming their input before committing. Reduces confirmation anxiety.
- **Effort-to-CTA color binding**: The warm orange of the slider thumb position bleeds into the CTA button color. The interface communicates "your selection is reflected here" without words.
- **Usual range visualization on slider**: Rather than a plain slider, a hatched zone marks the user's historical baseline. This gives personal context to an otherwise abstract scale — the user isn't just picking a number, they're locating themselves relative to their own history.
- **Floating badge on slider**: The "ABOVE USUAL RANGE" badge appears directly on the slider track rather than in a separate status area. Inline feedback at the point of interaction.
- **AI/smart default pill**: The "As Usual" button with sparkle icon suggests the system can auto-fill the effort field based on history. Placed inline, not as a banner — keeps the offer subtle and skippable.
- **Optional fields kept visible**: Rather than hiding non-required fields behind a disclosure, they appear as grayed-out rows. Signals completeness of the form while reducing pressure.
- **Qualitative description under effort label**: "Starting to feel the work, but still comfy enough to talk in shorter spurts" — translates the slider position into human language, making the abstract scale legible without numbers.

## Notes
- CTA as live summary — button subtitle mirrors current state, not static label
- Slider + personal baseline overlay = powerful personalization signal
- Color continuity: selection state → button color (effort orange = CTA orange)
- Hatched zone pattern for "normal range" — reusable for any baseline comparison UI
- Inline smart-fill pill (AI icon + label) — non-intrusive way to surface ML suggestions
- Qualitative copy under quantitative control = accessibility + warmth
- Optional fields as muted rows > hidden fields — transparency without friction
- Badge on thumb vs. status bar = feedback at point of contact

## Screen
A payments home screen from a peer-to-peer money transfer app. This is the main hub screen — early in the user journey, likely shown to a new or low-activity user. Layout is vertical scroll with a hero feature card dominating the upper portion, a balance widget below, and persistent action buttons above the nav bar.

## Layout & Spacing
Top section uses generous padding with a left-aligned avatar icon and headline. The hero card takes up roughly 55% of the visible screen height, creating strong visual hierarchy. A partially visible second card (the balance row) peeks below, signaling scrollability. The two CTA buttons are pinned just above the bottom nav — a persistent action zone separate from the scrollable content.

## Components
- **Hero feature card**: Large rounded-corner card in vivid blue. Contains stacked illustration, large white headline, supporting body copy, and an inset white pill button. Card clips slightly at right edge — hinting at a horizontal carousel.
- **Illustration cluster**: Three overlapping 3D-style objects (ball, shopping bag, coffee cup) — playful, lifestyle-oriented, not iconographic.
- **Inset CTA button**: White pill button inside the blue card — high contrast reversal treatment, semi-bold label in dark blue.
- **Balance row card**: White card with logo mark, large dollar amount, and label. Clean, minimal — functions as a status widget.
- **Dual action buttons**: Two equal-width pill buttons in golden yellow with dark bold labels. Persistent, floating above nav.
- **Bottom nav**: 3-tab bar, icon + label, active state in blue.

## Typography & Color
- Headline ("Pay someone"): ~24–26pt, white, medium-heavy weight
- Body copy: ~14pt, white, regular weight, centered
- Balance amount: ~22pt, dark, semi-bold — commanding without being decorative
- Section label ("Get started with..."): ~18pt, dark, left-aligned, sets context

Color palette: Royal blue (primary brand, hero card fill), golden yellow (action/CTA buttons), white (text on blue, card backgrounds), dark navy (text on light surfaces). Color is used functionally — blue = informational/feature, yellow = transactional action. Two-color CTA system creates clear visual grammar.

## Patterns
- **Peek carousel pattern**: Hero card bleeds slightly off right edge, implying horizontal swipe for more feature cards — reduces cognitive load by not showing all options at once.
- **Pinned dual-action bar**: "Send" and "Request" are always accessible regardless of scroll position — the two core actions never leave the screen. Unusual to pin these above nav rather than inside it.
- **Contextual onboarding card**: Rather than a modal or tooltip, feature education is embedded as a scrollable card with its own CTA — non-blocking, skippable, but prominent.
- **Illustration as emotional softener**: Using lifestyle objects (sports, shopping, coffee) instead of abstract icons makes a financial action feel casual and low-stakes — reduces payment anxiety.
- **Reversed button inside colored card**: White pill on blue background avoids the typical "ghost button on dark" legibility problem while maintaining visual harmony.
- **Balance as ambient information**: Zero balance shown without alarm or urgency — neutral display that doesn't guilt or pressure the user.

## Notes
- Pinned action pair above nav = always-accessible primary verbs, worth stealing for any 2-action utility app
- Peek card = low-friction way to surface multiple features without tabs or modals
- Yellow CTAs only for transactional actions — color as verb signal
- 3D illustration cluster > flat icons for warmth in fintech
- Inset white pill button inside colored card = elegant reversal pattern
- "Get started with..." framing = onboarding language that doesn't feel like onboarding
- Balance widget peeking below fold = scroll invitation without explicit prompt

- Brand toggle as pill with logos only → elegant multi-brand solution, no dropdown needed

- **Chartless axis**: The area chart intentionally omits gridlines, axis labels, and data point markers — optimizes for emotional read (trending up) over precise data analysis
- **Active state as shape change**: Time filter uses shape transformation (text → filled circle) rather than just color change to signal selection — more visually distinct and tactile-feeling
- **Collapsible grouped accounts**: Account types (like TFSA) act as accordion parents with sub-accounts nested inside — reduces cognitive load while preserving detail access
- **Notification on rewards, not profile**: Badge is on the gift icon rather than the profile — signals the app wants to surface rewards/referrals proactively, a deliberate engagement nudge
- **Layout density toggle**: The icon next to "Accounts" suggests user control over information density — respects different user mental models

## Notes
- Inline privacy toggle > buried in settings — steal this pattern for any balance/sensitive value
- Edge-to-edge chart with no axes = emotional data, not analytical; use when trend matters more than precision
- Shape-based active states (pill/circle) feel more premium than color-only changes
- Accordion accounts pattern: great for users with multiple sub-accounts under one tax wrapper
- Notification dot on rewards icon = clever engagement mechanic disguised as utility
- Loose vertical spacing in hero area signals "breathe, you're in control" — intentional calm UX for financial anxiety context

Generous white space in the upper-middle section creates a sense of "thinking room" — the emptiness is intentional, not a bug. The processing status block sits in the lower third, not centered, which feels natural as content that will grow upward. Source URLs are left-aligned with loose line-height, making them scannable. - **Source URL list**: Muted lavender/gray text, lowercase, progressively fading — uses "... and more" truncation pattern
- **Mode toggle pill**: Rounded pill with two icons (AI sparkle + search engine logo), left icon has white circular background indicating active state — floats above the gradient

- **Gradient as UI zone separator**: The purple-to-white gradient isn't decorative — it physically separates the answer zone from the control zone, acting as a soft modal floor
- **Mode toggle placement**: The AI/search toggle floats at the gradient boundary, making it feel like a persistent but non-intrusive control — accessible without being in the way
- **Query as title**: Displaying the user's query as a bold heading (not inside a search bar) reframes it as a document/session title, reinforcing the "answer" paradigm over "search"

## Notes
- Empty space during loading = trust signal, not poor UX — let it breathe
- Source list fade + truncation = elegant way to show depth without clutter
- Gradient floor trick for separating content from chrome — steal this
- "Reading X web pages" as microcopy pattern — quantify the work being done
- Toggle pill with active-state circle is cleaner than tab-style mode switching
- Query-as-title pattern removes search bar from result view entirely — reduces cognitive mode confusion
- Lavender toolbar bg ties bottom chrome to overall palette without being heavy

- **Challenge cards**: White cards with rounded corners, drop shadow, icon badge at top (hexagonal badge for branded challenge identity), green category label in small caps, social proof line ("456.3k active")
- **Bottom nav icons**: Circular icon treatment, active state uses filled red/coral circle background on icon

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Section headers ~18–20px semibold → Card headlines ~18px bold → Category labels small caps ~11px → Metadata ~13px regular gray
- **Color palette**: Deep royal blue, light powder blue (featured card), white card backgrounds, dark maroon CTA button, green category accent, coral/red nav active state
- **Functional color use**: Blue = featured/editorial content; Green = fasting/challenge category; Maroon CTA creates intentional contrast stop; Muted grays for secondary metadata

## Patterns
- **Split-panel card design**: Dividing a single card into two distinct color zones (dark + light) lets illustration and text coexist without competing — avoids the text-over-image legibility problem entirely
- **Content type labeling before title**: "ARTICLE" label above headline sets reader expectation before they read the title — reduces cognitive load on content type identification
- **Social proof on challenge cards**: "456.3k active" is embedded inline with duration metadata, normalizing participation data as a discovery signal rather than a separate badge
- **Peek-scroll pattern**: Third card partially visible signals scrollability without any explicit "swipe" instruction
- **Hexagonal badge as challenge identity**: Using a distinct shape (hex) for challenge icons differentiates them from generic circular avatars — creates a recognizable challenge visual language
- **Maroon/burgundy CTA on blue**: Unexpected warm-on-cool contrast for the CTA rather than white — more editorial, less app-generic

## Notes
- Split card = solve text-over-image problem architecturally, not with overlays
- Small-caps category label as content taxonomy — works harder than a colored dot
- Social proof as metadata, not badge — feels less gamified, more informational
- Hex badge shape = instant challenge category recognition system
- Warm CTA on cool background = editorial feel vs. typical white pill
- Active tab = filled pill, not just color change — stronger affordance signal
- Peek pattern needs only ~20–30px of next card to communicate scrollability

## Screen
A fitness activity summary screen from a health/activity tracking app. Shows daily movement data for a specific date. Sits within a daily detail view, accessed from a weekly overview. Layout flows top-to-bottom: date navigation → weekly ring strip → hero ring visualization → metric detail with chart → secondary stats grid.

---

## Layout & Spacing

Content is organized in clear horizontal bands with generous vertical breathing room. The hero ring takes roughly 40% of the screen height — a deliberate dominance choice. Below it, stats shift to a left-aligned text block with an inline bar chart. Secondary metrics (Steps, Distance) use a two-column grid. The bottom stat (Flights Climbed) sits as a single-column entry, implying a flexible grid that breaks when there's only one item. Tight spacing between label and value pairs creates strong visual grouping.

---

## Components

- **Weekly ring strip**: 7 small ring icons in a horizontal row, each representing a day. Active day highlighted with a colored dot above the letter; today's ring shown in accent color. Incomplete rings rendered as dark outlines.
- **Hero progress ring**: Large donut ring, ~60% screen width. Progress shown in vivid red-pink gradient with a rounded cap and an arrow icon embedded at the leading edge of the fill — doubles as a CTA affordance.
- **Inline bar chart**: Minimal, low-contrast bars on a black background. Dotted baseline in accent red. X-axis shows time labels; Y-axis has a single reference value. Very sparse — signal over noise.
- **Metric label + value pairs**: Label in small white regular weight, value in large bold with unit in smaller caps (e.g., "46/250CAL"). Goal denominator styled identically to current value — no visual de-emphasis.

---

## Typography & Color

**Type hierarchy:**
- Date header: medium weight, truncated with ellipsis — functional not decorative
- Metric labels: small, muted white, uppercase or sentence case
- Metric values: large, bold, high contrast — clear primary read
- Unit suffixes: smaller caps, same weight as value — integrated not separated

**Color palette:**
- Background: pure black
- Primary accent: vivid coral/red-pink (activity ring, chart bars, key values)
- Dark ring track: deep maroon/dark red — same hue family as accent, just very dark
- Secondary accent: chartreuse green (active tab indicator only)
- Text: white primary, muted gray secondary

Color is used functionally — red-pink = Move metric throughout (ring, chart, value). Green reserved exclusively for navigation active state, creating clear semantic separation.

---

## Patterns

**Arrow icon embedded in ring progress cap** — The leading edge of the ring fill contains a directional arrow, transforming a passive data viz into a tappable element. Elegant way to add interactivity hint without a separate button.

**Micro-ring strip as calendar nav** — Instead of a traditional date picker or calendar, the week is represented as 7 tiny versions of the hero metric. Reinforces the app's visual language and communicates progress at a glance without switching modes.

**Goal shown as denominator, not separate element** — "46/250CAL" presents current vs. goal as a fraction. No progress bar needed — the ring already handles that. Avoids redundancy while keeping goal visible.

**Dotted baseline on chart** — The bar chart uses a dotted red line as the zero/baseline instead of a solid axis, keeping it visually light while maintaining readability. Feels intentional, not default.

**Dark hue track for ring** — The empty portion of the ring isn't gray or neutral — it's a very dark version of the same red. Maintains color identity even in the "empty" state. Creates depth rather than absence.

**Single active color per metric** — All visual representations of the Move metric (ring, chart, value text) use the same exact red-pink. Strict color-to-metric mapping creates instant comprehension.

---

## Notes

- Arrow-in-ring-cap = interaction affordance + delight in one move
- Fraction format for goal (current/total) is cleaner than "X remaining" or separate goal label
- Dark track = same hue, extreme lightness reduction — try this instead of neutral gray tracks
- Micro data viz as nav element — rings as calendar days is a strong pattern for metric-forward apps
- Chartreuse green used ONLY for active nav — single-use accent color creates strong signal
- Bar chart with dotted baseline feels editorial/magazine — less dashboard, more story
- Type unit in small-caps inline with value — integrated unit treatment, not superscript
- Pure black bg makes the coral ring pop without any additional treatment — contrast does the work

## Typography & Color

**Type hierarchy**: Nav title is medium weight, centered. Section labels ("Ticketing," "Options") are small, muted, uppercase-adjacent — low contrast against background. - **Door code surfaced at creation**: Surfacing access/security info (door code) at the location step rather than burying it in settings is a thoughtful host-centric detail.

---

## Notes

- Tonal monochrome UI — one hue, multiple values = elegant depth without color noise
- Map embed as form validation UX — show don't tell
- Muted CTA = "form is fillable but not urgent" — explore this vs. disabled states
- Avatar in nav header for multi-host / account context — steal this pattern
- Icon prefix on every row = scannable form without reading labels
- Floating section labels (no box) feel editorial — try this instead of segmented headers
- Door code at location step = surface contextual secondary data where it belongs, not in settings
- Desaturated submit = softer than disabled, more honest than fully active

Health & fitness tracking dashboard — the home/summary screen of a calorie and activity tracking app. This is the primary landing screen after login, giving users a daily snapshot of nutrition, movement, and body metrics. ## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in a clear Z-pattern: header → hero card → supporting metric cards → secondary data cards. The hero calorie card takes dominant real estate (~40% of visible content area). Below it, a 2-column grid holds Steps and Exercise side-by-side. A horizontal scroll section (Weight, and a partially visible card) follows. ## Components

- **Hero Donut Chart Card** — Large white rounded card. Donut ring uses three colors (blue = consumed, orange = exercise bonus, gray = remaining). Center displays the key number large with a label below. Right side shows a vertical legend with icon + label + value rows.
- **Dot Pagination** — 4 dots below the hero card, first filled blue. Indicates swipeable card carousel without visible scroll affordance.
- **Metric Mini-Cards** — Two equal-width white cards in a row. Steps card includes a thin progress bar (pill shape, red fill at ~5%). Exercise card shows icon-paired stats stacked vertically.
- **Weight Card** — Full-width card with a + action in the top-right corner and a flat line chart starting to render — partially cut off to signal horizontal scroll.
- **Floating Search Bar** — Pill-shaped white input sitting above the nav bar on a bold blue background. Includes a barcode scanner icon on the right — a power-user affordance surfaced prominently.
- **Bottom Nav** — Blue background with white icons and labels. Active state uses a bold label only (no background pill or underline). Search bar is visually integrated into this zone, not separate.

## Typography & Color

**Type hierarchy:**
- Page title ("Today") — large, heavy, black
- Card titles ("Calories," "Steps") — medium weight, dark
- Primary data numbers — bold, large (~28–32px)
- Supporting labels — small, light gray, regular weight

**Color palette:**
- Primary blue (#2962FF range) — CTAs, active nav, bottom bar, donut ring
- Orange — exercise/bonus calorie indicator, fire emoji icon
- Red/coral — steps shoe icon, progress bar fill
- Gray — inactive donut segment, supporting text, inactive nav icons
- White — all card surfaces

Color is used functionally: blue = goal/progress, orange = earned/exercise, red = urgency/low progress. The bottom bar's bold blue creates a strong visual anchor and makes the search bar feel like a primary action, not an afterthought.

## Patterns

**Equation as subtitle** — "Remaining = Goal - Food + Exercise" is a formula shown as helper text under the card title. This is a rare pattern that teaches the user how the number is calculated inline, reducing confusion without requiring a tooltip or modal.

**Carousel hero with dot pagination** — The main metric is swipeable to reveal other summary views. Only dots indicate this — no visible swipe handles. Trusts the user to discover it, keeps the UI clean.

**Integrated search in nav zone** — The food search bar is embedded in the bottom navigation area rather than living in the content scroll. **Icon-as-category-label** — In the calorie legend, icons (flag, fork, flame) replace or supplement text labels. Reduces cognitive load for returning users who learn the visual vocabulary.

**Partially visible card as scroll invitation** — The weight card and a sibling card are cut off horizontally, signaling a swipeable row without any explicit "swipe" instruction.

**+ button as lightweight log action** — Small + icons on cards (Exercise, Weight) serve as contextual quick-add triggers without requiring navigation, keeping the user in the summary context.

---

## Notes
- Formula-as-subtitle is underused pattern — great for any calculated metric
- Barcode scanner in search = power feature surfaced at zero cost
- Blue nav bar + white pill search = nav as action zone, not just navigation
- Donut legend as vertical list with icons > traditional legend
- Partial card crop = horizontal scroll affordance without text instruction
- Consider: can the hero card carousel teach users about other metrics they haven't explored?
- Steps progress bar is almost empty (449/10k) — red fill color choice here is interesting, implies urgency without being alarming

## Screen
Health/fitness tracking dashboard — the home screen of a calorie and activity tracking app. This is the primary daily summary view users land on after opening the app. Layout is a single scrollable column with a persistent bottom bar containing a prominent search input and standard tab navigation.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in card-based rows with consistent horizontal margins (~16–20px). The hero card (calories) takes up roughly 40% of the visible content area. Below it, a two-column card row splits Steps and Exercise side-by-side at equal width. A third row begins (Weight/Sleep) and is deliberately cropped to signal scrollability. Pagination dots sit between the hero card and the secondary row, suggesting the hero area is horizontally swipeable. Generous internal card padding (~20px) keeps content from feeling cramped.

---

## Components
- **Hero donut chart card** — Large circular progress ring with multi-color segments (blue = consumed, orange = exercise bonus, gray = remaining). Center displays the key number prominently. Right side has a vertical legend with icon + label + value triplets.
- **Pagination dots** — 4 dots below the hero card; first dot filled blue, others gray. Indicates swipeable card carousel.
- **Two-column stat cards** — Equal-width cards with a bold label, emoji/icon, large number, and a thin linear progress bar (Steps card). Exercise card shows two icon+value rows.
- **Truncated bottom card row** — Partially visible cards hint at more content below.
- **Floating search bar** — Pill-shaped, white, embedded in the blue bottom bar above the nav. Includes a barcode scanner icon on the right.
- **Bottom nav bar** — Blue background, white icons and labels, active state shown by bold label only (no highlight pill or underline).
- **Edit button** — Small pill/chip button, light blue fill, right-aligned next to section title.

---

## Typography & Color
**Type hierarchy:**
- Section title ("Today") — large, heavy black
- Card titles ("Calories," "Steps") — medium bold black
- Primary metric numbers — large bold black
- Supporting labels — small regular gray
- Formula/subtitle text ("Remaining = Goal - Food + Exercise") — small, light gray, explanatory

**Color palette:**
- Primary blue (#3B5BDB-ish) — nav bar, active elements, ring fill, edit button
- Orange/amber — exercise calorie segment on ring, flame icon
- Red/coral — shoe icon, steps progress dot
- Gray — inactive ring segment, progress bar track, secondary text
- White — card backgrounds, search bar
- Color is used functionally: blue = progress/goal, orange = exercise/burn, red = activity metrics

---

## Patterns
- **Contextual formula as subtitle** — "Remaining = Goal - Food + Exercise" teaches the user the calculation logic inline, reducing confusion without a help modal. Elegant onboarding-in-context.
- **Donut chart with external legend** — Rather than labeling segments directly on the ring, a vertical icon-legend to the right keeps the chart clean while providing full detail. Icons (flag, fork, flame) add scannability without text overload.
- **Persistent contextual search elevated above nav** — The food search bar lives in the nav zone but above the tab bar, making the most frequent action (logging food) immediately accessible from the home screen without a FAB or modal trigger.
- **Barcode scanner inside search pill** — Secondary input method embedded directly in the search component; no separate button or menu needed.
- **Carousel hero with dots** — The main summary widget is swipeable to reveal other macro/nutrient views, keeping the dashboard clean while hiding depth.
- **Partial card crop as scroll affordance** — The bottom card row is intentionally cut off to communicate scrollability without any explicit "see more" label.
- **Premium badge in header** — Crown icon + "PREMIUM" label subtly placed under the logo signals tier without a banner or interstitial.

---

## Notes
- Formula-as-subtitle = great pattern for any calculated metric dashboard
- Donut + side legend combo > labeled segments for small rings
- Persistent search in nav zone = zero-step access to primary action
- Barcode icon in search pill — dual input affordance, no extra UI
- Crop bottom cards intentionally — scroll hint without copy
- Two-col stat cards work well for paired metrics (activity + exercise)
- Blue nav bar with white icons — high contrast, no active pill needed if bold label is enough
- Edit chip button (pill, light blue) = clean alternative to gear icon for dashboard customization

## Screen
Ride-hailing service selection screen. Sits in the booking flow after destination is set — user is choosing a vehicle tier before confirming. Layout splits vertically: top ~55% is a live map, bottom ~45% is a scrollable bottom sheet with ride options. Primary interactions are tier selection, CTA button, and payment method selector.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Classic map + bottom sheet split. The bottom sheet uses a drag indicator pill and floats over the map with a promo banner acting as a visual bridge/header between the two zones. Ride options use consistent row height with generous internal padding (~16px vertical). Selected state breaks the grid slightly with a bordered card that adds visual weight. Bottom is anchored with a fixed payment row + CTA — clean separation from scrollable content above.

---

## Components
- **Search bar (top):** Pill-shaped, white, with back arrow left and edit icon right. Destination shown as a tag/chip within the field
- **Map:** Full-bleed with route line, animated car cluster near origin, green "2 min" bubble on origin pin, and a destination callout bubble ("Arrive by 8:41") in blue
- **Promo banner:** Full-width indigo/blue strip with checkmark + expandable chevron — sits between map and sheet
- **Ride tier rows:** Horizontal list items — 3D car illustration left, label + metadata center, discounted price pair right (current price bold, original struck through)
- **Selected tier card:** Green outline border, slightly elevated visual treatment, badge icon next to label (signal/quality indicator)
- **Payment row:** Two avatar-style icon buttons (person + bag) + payment method label with dropdown chevron
- **CTA button:** Large rounded pill, green, context-aware label ("Select Economy"); paired with a secondary square-rounded icon button (schedule/clock) in matching green

---

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy:** Tier name in medium-bold ~16px; metadata (time, capacity) in small gray ~12px; price in bold ~16px; struck-through original price in smaller gray
- **Color palette:** White sheet background, indigo/blue for promo strip and map route, green for selected state + CTA + origin pin, gray for unselected content
- **Functional color use:** Green = selected/actionable/positive; blue = navigational/informational; strikethrough gray = savings contrast; indigo banner draws eye to discount before pricing

---

## Patterns
- **Dual-price display with strikethrough:** Discount made viscerally visible at point of comparison — every row shows savings, not just the promo banner. Reinforces the promo banner's claim at the item level
- **Contextual CTA labeling:** Button reads "Select Economy" not just "Confirm" — mirrors the selected option dynamically, reducing cognitive load and confirming selection state
- **3D isometric car illustrations per tier:** Not flat icons — gives each tier personality and differentiation beyond just a name, making scanning feel more like browsing
- **Promo banner as sheet header:** The discount strip acts as a visual anchor between map and content, and its expandable chevron suggests more detail without cluttering the list
- **Arrive-by callout on map:** Instead of just showing destination pin, a speech-bubble callout shows ETA directly on the map — connects the abstract route to a concrete time promise
- **Animated car cluster near origin:** Multiple car icons near pickup give a sense of real availability/proximity, building trust before the user even reads wait times
- **Schedule button paired with CTA:** Secondary action (book for later) lives as an icon button beside the primary CTA — same visual weight family but doesn't compete

---

## Notes
- Dual price on every row > single promo callout — make savings feel systemic not gimmicky
- 3D car art per tier = worth the asset investment for differentiation
- Green border selection state on list items — subtle but clear without background fill
- "Arrive by X" bubble on map is underused pattern — great for trust-building
- Promo banner as sheet divider is clever real estate use
- Context-aware CTA copy should be standard on any selection→confirm flow
- Payment method row between list and CTA = good friction reducer (visible before commit)

## Screen
Dating app profile feed screen — the main discovery/browsing view. Users scroll through individual profiles presented as cards. Sits at the core of the app experience, between onboarding and messaging. Layout is a single-column vertical scroll with a filter bar at top, an onboarding nudge banner, profile header info, and a large photo card with inline action.

## Layout & Spacing
Top filter bar sits flush with generous horizontal padding. Below it, a soft lavender nudge banner spans full width with rounded corners and comfortable internal padding (~16px). Profile name/status sits outside the card as a header, creating a two-part profile unit: metadata above, media below. Photo card is nearly full-width with tight corner radius. A second media card (video) begins to peek at the bottom, signaling scrollability. The like button floats inside the photo at bottom-right — anchored to the image rather than the chrome.

## Components
- **Filter chips**: Outlined pill buttons with chevrons; the active/selected state uses a visible border stroke to distinguish from inactive. Accompanied by a sliders icon for full filter access.
- **Nudge banner**: Rounded rectangle, lavender fill, icon in a circular container left-aligned, two-line copy. Soft, non-intrusive coaching moment.
- **Profile header**: Name in large bold weight, inline green presence dot + "Active now" label, verified badge with checkmark icon below name. Three-dot overflow and a loop/undo icon sit right-aligned.
- **Photo card**: Full-bleed image, rounded corners, floating heart button (circular, white fill, bottom-right of image).
- **Video card**: Partially visible, dark overlay, timestamp badge top-right, large dismiss X button (dark circle, bottom-left overlap).
- **Bottom nav**: Five icons — home, star, heart, chat, profile avatar thumbnail.

## Typography & Color
- Name: ~28–30px, heavy/black weight, dark
- "Active now": ~13px, medium weight, muted — green dot for presence
- "Verified": ~13px, medium, paired with purple badge icon
- Banner copy: Two-line hierarchy — bold headline + regular subtext, both small-medium size
- Timestamp on video: Monospaced-feel, small, white on dark
- Color palette: Predominantly white/light background, purple/lavender as brand accent (banner, badge, heart icon), green for presence, dark near-black for text and nav. Heart actions use a warm rose/pink.

## Patterns
- **Floating in-image action**: The like/heart button lives inside the photo card itself rather than below it — reduces thumb travel and contextually ties the action to the content being reacted to.
- **Presence signal inline with name**: "Active now" is embedded in the name row rather than a separate section, making it feel like a natural attribute rather than a status indicator.
- **Peek scroll affordance**: The video card is intentionally cropped at the bottom edge, communicating that profiles contain multiple media items without any explicit instruction.
- **Coaching banner as passive nudge**: The "start sending likes" prompt is styled as an informational card, not a modal or alert — it teaches behavior without interrupting flow.
- **Verification as trust signal near name**: Badge placed directly under the name (not in a corner or tooltip) makes credibility immediately scannable.
- **Dismiss X on video overlaps card boundary**: The close button bleeds outside the video card frame, making it feel grabbable and reducing accidental taps on the content itself.
- **Filter bar with icon + chips**: Combining a settings/sliders icon with individual attribute chips lets power users go deep while casual users can filter by single dimensions quickly.

## Notes
- Like btn inside image = action belongs to content, not UI chrome
- Presence dot + name inline → profile feels "live"
- Lavender nudge banner = brand color doing double duty as coaching UI
- Peek pattern for multi-media profiles — no dots/indicators needed
- Verified badge below name, not overlaid on photo — cleaner trust signal
- Video dismiss btn oversized + offset = intentional fat-finger protection
- Filter chips: outlined = unselected, could explore filled = selected state
- Avatar in nav = quick profile access without label

## Screen
Dating app discovery/feed screen. This is the main browsing interface where users scroll through profiles. Layout flows top-to-bottom: filter bar → onboarding nudge banner → profile card with name/status → full-width photo → video preview card. Vertical scroll feed rather than swipe-card paradigm.

## Layout & Spacing
Content uses full-width cards edge-to-edge within the screen. The profile photo bleeds fully to screen edges creating an immersive feel. Name and metadata sit above the photo rather than overlaid on it — a deliberate legibility choice. The onboarding prompt banner sits between filters and profile content, using a soft lavender fill to distinguish it from the white background without being aggressive. Generous breathing room between the filter row and banner. Video card partially peeks at bottom, signaling scrollability.

## Components
- **Filter chips**: Pill-shaped with outline border, chevron dropdown indicators, left-aligned icon for settings. Clean, minimal weight.
- **Onboarding nudge banner**: Rounded rectangle, muted purple/lavender fill, circular icon container with heart, two-line copy. Feels informational not promotional.
- **Verified badge**: Small purple shield with checkmark inline with name — compact trust signal.
- **Active status indicator**: Small green dot + "Active now" text inline with name. Subtle presence signal.
- **Like button on photo**: Floating circular button, bottom-right of image, white background with heart icon — contextual action anchored to content.
- **Video preview card**: Dark overlay card with timestamp (0:11) top-right, dismiss X button prominent bottom-left. Peek layout implies scroll-to-reveal.
- **Bottom nav**: Five icons — home, favorites/star, like/heart, comment, profile avatar.

## Typography & Color
Strong typographic hierarchy: large bold serif-ish name at ~28pt, smaller regular weight status text, small caption for verified. Name is the clear anchor. Color palette is restrained — white background, near-black text, purple/violet as the single accent color used for: verified badge, active heart icon in banner, filter chip border. Green used exclusively for presence dot. Photo is black-and-white, which keeps color palette clean and focused.

## Patterns
- **Metadata above image, not overlaid**: Avoids the common dark-gradient-over-photo pattern. Name/status are always legible regardless of photo content.
- **Contextual like button on photo**: Heart action is placed directly on the photo rather than only in a bottom action bar — reduces friction, keeps action spatially connected to content.
- **Onboarding nudge as feed element**: The "start sending likes" prompt is embedded inline in the feed flow rather than as a modal or toast. It's dismissible by scrolling past it, not by tapping X — low interruption.
- **Peek-scroll affordance**: The video card at bottom is deliberately cropped to signal there's more content below without any explicit "scroll" instruction.
- **Dual action surfaces**: Like can happen via the floating photo button OR the bottom nav heart — redundant paths reduce missed interactions.
- **Presence signal inline with identity**: "Active now" sits next to the name at the same visual level as the name, making it feel like a core identity attribute rather than a status badge.

## Notes
- Name above photo = always readable, no gradient hacks needed
- Lavender nudge banner = soft enough to feel helpful not pushy
- Verified badge earns its space by being tiny + inline, not a separate row
- Video peek = scroll bait done tastefully
- Two like entry points = smart redundancy for core action
- Monochrome photos keep UI color language clean and intentional
- Active status as inline text dot combo = efficient presence UI

## Screen
Food delivery app home screen — the primary discovery/browse surface users land on after opening the app. Layout flows top-to-bottom: location header → search bar → hero banner → category scroll → filter chips → restaurant grid. This is the main feed, not a category or search results page.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in distinct horizontal bands with clear visual separation. The hero banner bleeds edge-to-edge creating a full-width moment before returning to contained card grids. Category icons use a horizontal scroll with food photography thumbnails above text labels. Restaurant cards sit in a 3-column grid (partially visible, implying scroll-right). Filter chips run in a single horizontal scroll row between categories and results — acting as a transitional layer between browsing and filtering.

## Components
- **Location selector**: Bold primary location name + secondary truncated address + dropdown chevron. Two-line treatment signals precision without cluttering.
- **Avatar cluster**: Two overlapping circular avatars (one with play button overlay) + coin/wallet icon + profile initial — compact utility row top-right.
- **Search bar**: Rounded pill with ghost text using a specific food item ("bread") as contextual placeholder. Mic icon on right for voice input.
- **VEG MODE toggle**: Isolated in its own labeled box adjacent to search — green toggle, distinct container, high visibility placement.
- **Hero banner**: Pink gradient background with illustrated delivery bag, bold typographic hierarchy (promo label > headline > subtext), ribbon-style "FREE DELIVERY" badge.
- **Category scroll**: Circular food photography thumbnails above labels. Active state = red underline on text label. First item is a promotional "50% OFF" tile styled differently (yellow/red badge, no photo).
- **Filter chips**: Pill-shaped, outlined. "New" badge embedded inside the Schedule chip — badge within chip is an interesting nested component.
- **Restaurant cards**: Image fills top ~70%, rating badge overlaid bottom-left (dark pill with star), discount label top-left (dark overlay), name + delivery time below image.

## Typography & Color
Strong hierarchy: location name is heaviest/largest in header, section labels ("RECOMMENDED FOR YOU") in spaced caps at reduced weight. Card names are medium-weight, delivery time is light/small. Primary brand color is red/coral used on active states, CTAs, and badges. Pink used as warm background tone for hero. Yellow used for the third-party grocery tab (distinct brand color preserved). Discount labels use dark semi-transparent overlays on images for legibility without hard backgrounds.

## Patterns
- **Promo tile as first category item**: The "50% OFF Explore" tile is styled as a category chip but functions as a promotional CTA — blends discovery with merchandising without a separate banner slot.
- **Badge-within-chip**: The "New" label embedded inside the Schedule filter chip signals recency of a feature without adding a separate UI element or tooltip.
- **Contextual search placeholder**: Using "bread" instead of generic "Search restaurants" implies the search is food-item aware, setting user expectation for item-level search capability.
- **VEG MODE as a persistent global filter**: Placed adjacent to search rather than buried in filters — treats dietary preference as a first-class, always-accessible toggle rather than a filter option.
- **Third-party tab with brand color preserved**: The grocery delivery tab at the bottom nav retains its own brand yellow/black identity within the host app's nav — signals partnership rather than full integration, building trust through brand recognition.
- **Overlapping avatar UI**: The play-button overlay on the profile avatar suggests a live/story feature accessible from the home screen without dedicated nav real estate.
- **Rating badge placement**: Bottom-left of card image rather than below — keeps the card compact and lets rating be seen before the eye reaches the text metadata.

## Notes
- Promo as category item = smart merchandising without extra layout cost
- VEG MODE toggle placement = dietary filters deserve top-level real estate in relevant markets
- Nested badge inside chip — useful for "new feature" signaling without tooltips
- Contextual placeholder copy = sets search capability expectations
- Preserve partner brand colors in nav tabs to signal trust
- Overlapping circular avatars for social/live features = compact social entry point
- Dark overlay discount labels on images > colored text boxes for visual cleanliness
- Two-line location header: bold primary / light secondary works well for address hierarchy

## Screen
A micro-mobility rental app's map view with an active vehicle type filter panel. The screen sits at the core discovery/browsing stage of the rental flow — before a user selects a specific vehicle. Layout is layered: full-bleed map as base, a floating card panel overlaid at the top, and a persistent action bar at the bottom.

---

## Layout & Spacing
The filter card floats over the map with generous internal padding (~24px sides), creating clear separation from the map content beneath. The two vehicle type options are centered horizontally with equal weight. The bottom action bar uses a split pill layout — one primary CTA (left, ~60% width) and one secondary (right, ~38%). Map markers are distributed naturally across the canvas with no forced grid.

---

## Components

- **Filter card:** White rounded card (large radius ~20px), elevated with subtle shadow. Sits top-center with a title, two icon selectors, and flanking utility buttons.
- **Vehicle type selectors:** Circular icon containers. Unselected = light gray fill, low-opacity icon. Selected = white fill with a bold green circular stroke border. Label below in small regular text.
- **Map markers:** Small white circles with green dashed/solid stroke borders containing vehicle icons. Dashed border = cluster or lower priority; solid = individual available unit. Varying sizes suggest proximity or zoom hierarchy.
- **Primary CTA pill:** Soft green fill, white icon + label, fully rounded. Slightly larger than secondary.
- **Secondary CTA pill:** White fill, black stroke border, black icon + label, fully rounded. Matches height of primary.
- **Location FAB:** Small white circle button, bottom-right of map, with arrow/location icon.
- **Header utility buttons:** Left = profile icon (circle, gray), Right = close X (circle, gray). Both match in size and treatment.

---

## Typography & Color

- **Type hierarchy:** "Vehicle types" is medium-weight title (~17px). Vehicle labels ("Bikes," "Scooters") are small regular (~13px), subordinate. CTA labels are medium-weight (~15px). Map POI labels are system-default small gray.
- **Color palette:**
  - Green (#6DCE3F-ish): selection indicator, primary CTA fill, marker borders — single brand color doing all active/interactive work
  - White: card surface, marker fills, button fills
  - Light gray: unselected states, icon backgrounds, map base
  - Black/dark: icons, secondary button text, map labels
- Color is used with strict functional discipline — green = active/selected/primary action only.

---

## Patterns

- **Inline contextual filter panel over map:** Rather than a modal or bottom sheet, the filter appears as a card anchored to the top of the map. Keeps spatial context visible while filtering — user sees the map update in real time beneath.
- **Opacity-dimmed unselected state:** The unselected vehicle icon (bike) is visually faded/ghosted, making the active selection immediately legible without removing the option from view.
- **Dashed vs. solid marker rings:** A subtle encoding system — dashed circles likely indicate clustered or lower-confidence positions; solid circles indicate confirmed single units. Communicates data quality spatially without tooltips.
- **Split pill bottom bar:** Two actions of different visual weight share the same horizontal zone. The asymmetric width split (primary wider) reinforces hierarchy without stacking.
- **Profile access from filter header:** Placing the profile icon in the filter card header (rather than a nav bar) is an unconventional placement that saves chrome while keeping it accessible.
- **Marker sizing variation on map:** Larger marker circles appear to indicate clusters or proximity, creating a lightweight visual clustering system without numeric badges.

---

## Notes
- Green ring = selected, gray fill = unselected — dead simple toggle pattern, steal this
- Dashed marker border as "soft" state — great for uncertain/clustered data
- Filter card floats over map = no context lost during filtering
- Split pill bar: ~60/40 width split signals priority without color alone
- Ghost/dim unselected icons instead of hiding them — keeps affordance visible
- Single accent color doing ALL interactive work — very clean
- Profile in filter header = unconventional but saves nav real estate
- Map stays live under filter card — no overlay dimming, intentional

## Screen
Micro-mobility rental app — map view with an active vehicle type filter panel. This sits on the main discovery screen, likely triggered by a filter/type toggle. The layout is a full-bleed map with a floating modal card overlaid at the top, and persistent action buttons anchored to the bottom.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct z-layers: map base, floating filter card (top), floating CTAs (bottom). The filter card uses generous internal padding with two centered icon options side-by-side. Map pins scatter organically beneath. Bottom bar splits into two asymmetric pills — one wider (primary), one narrower (secondary). The filter card doesn't cover the full screen, preserving map context underneath.

---

## Components

- **Filter card**: White rounded card, elevated with subtle shadow. Contains header row (icon + title + close X), and two icon-selector circles below with labels.
- **Vehicle type selectors**: Circular icon containers. Unselected = light gray fill, muted icon. Selected = white fill with bold green ring border. Real product image used inside circle.
- **Map pins**: Small white circles with scooter icon + green dashed circular border — dashed stroke signals availability/active state. Clustered pins shown as grouped (two icons in one elongated pill shape).
- **Primary CTA ("My group")**: Wide pill button, light green fill, dark icon + label. Soft, pastel — not aggressive.
- **Secondary CTA ("Scan")**: White pill button, outlined, scan-frame icon + label.
- **Location button**: Circular white floating button, bottom-right, arrow icon.

---

## Typography & Color

- **Type hierarchy**: Card title is medium-weight, centered (~16–17px). Selector labels are small, regular weight beneath icons. Map labels use standard map type (light gray, small).
- **Color palette**: White (#FFFFFF) dominant UI surface. Green (#4CAF50-adjacent) as the single brand accent — used on selected ring, map pin borders, primary button fill. Gray for unselected states and map base. No secondary accent colors — extremely restrained.
- **Color function**: Green = selected/active/primary action. Gray = inactive/neutral. White = surface. The consistency of green across selection state, map pins, and CTA creates strong visual coherence.

---

## Patterns

- **Dashed circle as "available" signal**: Map pins use a dashed green ring rather than solid — visually communicates "ready to grab" without text. Clever use of stroke style as status encoding.
- **Partial modal over map**: Filter card overlays only the top portion, keeping the live map visible. User maintains spatial context while filtering — avoids full-screen takeover for a lightweight decision.
- **Image-in-circle selector**: Using actual product photography inside the selector circle (not flat icons) makes vehicle type immediately recognizable and tactile. Adds premium feel to a simple binary choice.
- **Asymmetric bottom CTA pair**: Primary action gets ~60% width with color fill; secondary gets ~40% with outline treatment. Side-by-side pills instead of stacked — efficient use of horizontal space, clear hierarchy without burying secondary action.
- **Grouped pin treatment**: Multiple nearby vehicles shown as a merged elongated pill with two icons — avoids pin clutter without losing count information.
- **Muted primary button**: The "My group" button uses a soft pastel green rather than saturated — feels friendly rather than urgent, appropriate for a social/group feature.

---

## Notes
- Dashed ring = availability metaphor — steal for any "ready state" map pin
- Real product photo in selector circle >> flat icon for tangible goods
- Partial overlay modal keeps map alive — better than full sheet for spatial apps
- Pastel CTA for social features, saturated for transactional — intentional tone split
- Single accent color across all interactive states = strong visual system discipline
- Elongated cluster pill with dual icons — elegant density solution for map crowding

## Screen
A crypto/digital wallet dashboard — the home screen of a personal finance or digital asset app. Sits at the top of the main navigation flow. Layout flows top-to-bottom: personalized header → total balance hero → sparkline chart with time filters → 2×2 account type grid → dismissible promotional banner → bottom nav with FAB.

---

## Layout & Spacing

Content is organized in clear horizontal bands with generous vertical breathing room between sections. The chart section has notable negative space above it, letting the balance number breathe. The 2×2 grid uses equal-sized cards with consistent internal padding — icon top-left, label and value bottom-left. The banner sits as a distinct section below the grid, visually separated. Bottom nav is sparse (3 icons + FAB), keeping the tab bar minimal.

---

## Components

- **Balance display** — oversized bold numeral, dollars in heavy black, cents in lighter gray/reduced weight. Creates a natural visual split within a single number.
- **Performance badge** — small green pill with upward arrow icon + "100%" label, sits inline with "Total Balance" label. Compact, color-coded status chip.
- **Sparkline chart** — ultra-minimal, low-contrast dotted/dashed baseline with a single vertical marker line at the right edge. Almost ghosted into the background.
- **Time range selector** — plain text tabs, selected state is bold weight only (no pill, no underline, no background). Extremely subtle active state.
- **Account cards (2×2 grid)** — white rounded-rect cards, soft shadow. App-icon-style logos (rounded square icons) top-left. Label in gray, value in black below. One card ("Fuse Card") is a CTA state — bold label + subtitle instead of a dollar value.
- **Promotional banner** — white card with rounded corners, icon, headline + subtext, dismissible X, and pagination dots. Carousel indicator shows 2 slides.
- **FAB** — large black circle with white plus, positioned right of bottom nav, elevated.

---

## Typography & Color

**Type hierarchy:**
1. Balance: ~40px, heavy/black weight
2. Card values: ~18px, medium weight
3. Section labels / card labels: ~13px, gray, light weight
4. Banner text: ~15px headline bold, ~13px subtext gray

**Color palette:**
- Background: off-white/light gray (#F2F2F2 range)
- Cards: pure white
- Primary text: near-black
- Secondary text: medium gray
- Accent green: used only for the positive performance badge
- Icon colors: each account type has its own brand color (blue, orange, purple, black) — color is used to differentiate asset categories, not as a global UI accent

---

## Patterns

- **Split decimal typography** — dollars rendered in full black weight, cents in reduced opacity/gray. Draws the eye to the significant figure while keeping precision visible. Reused consistently across all card values.
- **Icon-as-category-identifier** — each account card uses a distinct colored app-style icon rather than a generic category icon. Makes accounts feel like distinct "apps" within the wallet, aiding recognition over recall.
- **CTA card disguised as data card** — the "Fuse Card" slot uses the same card component as balance cards but substitutes a value for a descriptive CTA string. Upsell feels native, not intrusive.
- **Ghost chart** — the sparkline is intentionally low-contrast and minimal, functioning more as ambient context than a data-dense visualization. Avoids overwhelming the balance hero.
- **Weight-only active state** — the time range selector uses font weight alone to signal selection, with no background or color change. Very restrained; works because the options are short and few.
- **Dismissible banner with pagination** — the promotional slot is paginated and closeable, signaling that it's temporary/optional content rather than core UI. Reduces perceived pushiness.
- **Personalized screen title** — using the user's name in the header ("Sam's Wallet") creates ownership and context without requiring an avatar or profile element.

---

## Notes

- Split decimal color trick → steal for any financial balance display
- App-icon style category logos = strong wayfinding, consider for multi-account dashboards
- Ghost sparkline = "ambient data" pattern — context without cognitive load
- Weight-only tab selection = ultra-minimal, only works with short label sets
- CTA-as-grid-card = native upsell pattern, no banner blindness
- FAB + sparse 3-icon nav = action-forward layout, primary action always thumb-reachable
- Personalized title as ownership signal — cheap but effective trust/engagement cue

## Screen

Full-screen immersive layout with no hard containers — content floats over a blurred gradient background. Text block is positioned in the lower-middle third, creating generous negative space above (almost 60% of screen height is empty/atmospheric). Input mode buttons cluster near the bottom of the content area, just above the nav. The asymmetry of top-heavy empty space feels intentional — calming, meditative pacing.

- **Greeting text**: Personalized small-weight line above the main prompt, creates a two-tier text block
- **Main prompt**: Large, bold, lowercase serif-adjacent sans — the hero element of the screen
- **Sparkle/add button**: Small circular button with a 4-pointed star icon directly below the prompt — likely triggers AI suggestions or prompts
- **Input mode selectors**: Three frosted-glass circular buttons (type / yap / speak) with labels beneath, center-aligned. Middle "yap" button is noticeably larger, indicating primary action
- **FAB (+)**: White pill/circle button floating at bottom-right, overlapping the nav bar boundary
- **Bottom nav**: Dark bar with 4 icon+label tabs, high contrast against the content area above

## Typography & Color
All type is white on the atmospheric background. Two-weight hierarchy: small regular weight for greeting/labels, large bold lowercase for the prompt question. Lowercase styling throughout creates an informal, conversational tone. Color palette: muted blue-gray in the upper half bleeding into warm taupe/brown in the lower half — a gradient that feels like dusk or dawn. Functional color use is minimal; the larger center button uses slightly different opacity to signal primacy.

## Patterns
- **Tri-modal input affordance**: Offering type / voice-AI / voice as three distinct entry modes in one view is a strong multimodal pattern — the center "yap" option (likely AI-assisted voice) is elevated in size, signaling a preferred/novel interaction without hiding alternatives
- **Lowercase conversational copy**: All UI text uses lowercase, including nav labels and prompts — a deliberate tone choice that reduces formality and matches the reflective/intimate context
- **Atmospheric full-bleed background as mood-setting**: The blurred gradient isn't decorative — it creates an emotional state before the user types anything. The warm-to-cool gradient mirrors time of day or emotional temperature
- **Prompt-first architecture**: No dashboard, no stats, no streaks on the home screen — the entire screen is dedicated to a single question, reducing friction to zero
- **Floating sparkle CTA below prompt**: Placing an AI/suggestions trigger directly adjacent to the prompt text (not in a toolbar) creates contextual discoverability — it reads as "need help with this?" without explanation
- **FAB crossing nav boundary**: The white + button overlaps the nav bar edge, creating visual continuity between content and navigation zones while maintaining clear affordance

## Notes
- Lowercase everywhere = personality, not laziness — try this on emotional/wellness apps
- Three input modes with size hierarchy > tabs or dropdowns for mode switching
- Huge negative space above fold = breathing room as UX, not wasted space
- Gradient bg does emotional work — don't underestimate bg as mood tool
- "Yap" as copy for AI voice = playful naming reduces intimidation of new interaction model
- Sparkle icon below prompt = inline AI nudge, not buried in settings
- FAB overlapping nav = blurs zones intentionally, worth trying when content + action are tightly linked

## Layout & Spacing
Content sits in a modal card with generous top padding before the input field. The suggested headlines are left-aligned with consistent vertical rhythm (~40px rows), each paired with a right-aligned icon. The toolbar lives in a fixed strip directly above the keyboard — a practical floating action bar that stays anchored to the input context. Horizontal margins are tight and consistent throughout.

## Components
- **Search input field** — Large, borderless, placeholder text in muted blue-gray. Cursor visible, no background fill. Feels more like a document than a form field.
- **Dismiss button (X)** — Small circular icon button, top-left, minimal weight. Provides escape without visual dominance.
- **Trending/suggested rows** — Plain text list items with no dividers, muted gray color, each with a diagonal arrow-out icon (↗ style) indicating "go to this topic." No chips, no thumbnails.
- **Toolbar strip** — Contains: globe icon, plus icon, "Search" pill/dropdown (with chevron), microphone icon, waveform/audio icon. Compact, icon-forward.

## Typography & Color
- **Input placeholder** — Large (~28–30pt), light weight, blue-gray tint. Conversational tone ("Ask anything...") signals AI/natural language input.

## Patterns
- **Trending content as zero-state** — Rather than leaving the search field empty or showing a generic prompt, real current headlines populate the space. This makes the empty state feel alive and useful, nudging users toward engagement without explicit CTAs.
- **Diagonal arrow icon on suggestions** — Using a northeast arrow (↗) instead of a search icon or chevron signals "jump to this topic" — a subtle distinction that implies the item is a shortcut, not a search refinement.
- **Mode-switching pill above keyboard** — The "Search ↓" dropdown suggests the same input field serves multiple modes (search vs. AI ask vs. browse). Hiding complexity behind a single pill keeps the UI clean while preserving power-user flexibility.
- **Toolbar anchored above keyboard** — Placing action tools (voice, web, attach) in the strip above the keyboard rather than in the header keeps them thumb-accessible exactly when needed — only visible during active input.
- **Conversational placeholder copy** — "Ask anything..." frames the interaction as dialogue, not database query. This is a deliberate tone choice that shapes user mental model.

---

## Notes
- Zero-state = live trending content → never feels empty
- Suppress suggestion contrast intentionally — ambient, not competing
- ↗ arrow = "teleport to topic" not "refine search" — icon choice carries meaning
- Mode pill above keyboard = elegant complexity hiding
- Toolbar-above-keyboard pattern worth stealing for any chat/compose UI
- No submit button = strong AI signal, changes user behavior
- Placeholder as UX copy — "Ask anything" does real work
- Full-bleed white card + system keyboard = seamless depth illusion
