- **"New" badge on tab label**: Draws attention to a feature/section without interrupting the flow — embedded inline in the navigation rather than a modal or tooltip. Dismissible by familiarity.
- **Gold star as editorial signal**: The yellow star in the metadata row is the only warm color on screen. It functions as a visual "editor's pick" or boost marker — users learn to associate it with quality content without explicit labeling.
- **Publication + author attribution chain**: "In [Publication] by [Author]" — two-level attribution in a single line. Useful for platforms with both personal and publication-based content. Lets users follow either entity.
- **Dismiss control inline with metadata**: The minus-circle button lives in the same row as engagement stats, not in a swipe gesture or hidden menu. Surfaces a "not interested" action without requiring gesture discovery.
- **`+` tab for customization**: Placing the add-tab affordance directly in the tab row (not in settings) makes feed personalization feel immediate and low-friction.
- **Hairline dividers over card shadows**: Keeps the feed feeling like a reading list/editorial layout rather than a card-based app — appropriate for a text-first platform.
- **Profile avatar with badge in bottom nav**: Combines account access with notification state in one element — reduces nav bar icon count while maintaining status visibility.

---

## Notes
- Star icon = instant editorial hierarchy signal, no label needed
- "In [pub] by [author]" attribution pattern — steal for multi-entity content platforms
- Inline dismiss > swipe-to-dismiss for discoverability
- New badge on tab = lightweight feature announcement pattern
- Achromatic feed + single accent = content-first, UI recedes
- Hairlines not cards = editorial not app-y
- FAB green matches badge green — tight accent discipline
- `+` in tab row = personalization without leaving context

## Screen
AI companion/conversational app home screen. This is the entry point — a mode-selection or conversation-starter hub. Users pick a conversational intent before engaging. Layout is a scrollable vertical feed of 2-column card pairs, topped with a large display heading and profile icon, with a persistent input bar pinned to the bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Two-column card grid with generous equal gutters (~12–16px). Each card is tall (roughly square image + text label below), creating a magazine-editorial feel. The heading sits flush left with significant top breathing room. Cards appear to scroll infinitely, with the bottom row clipping to hint at more content. The sticky input bar floats above the scroll content, clearly separated.

## Components
- **Mode cards**: Rounded rectangle cards (~16px radius) with a top image block and a cream/off-white lower text area. Image takes ~55% of card height. Text label is left-aligned, multi-line, large serif.
- **Card images**: Illustrated/painterly art style — warm, editorial illustrations rather than photography. Each image is unique and thematically matched to its label.
- **Header**: Large serif display text, left-aligned. Profile avatar button top-right — circular, muted tone, icon-only.
- **Input bar**: Full-width pill/rounded bar pinned to bottom. Contains logo mark left, placeholder text center, and a teal/green circular call-to-action button (phone icon) right.

## Typography & Color
- **Display heading**: Large serif, dark forest green — dominant, editorial weight
- **Card labels**: Medium serif, same dark green, 2–3 lines, comfortable leading
- **Color palette**: Warm cream/off-white background (#F5F0E8 range), deep forest green for all text, muted sage and terracotta accents in illustrations. The CTA button is a saturated teal-green — the only high-contrast accent color on screen.
- Color is used with extreme restraint: one accent color (teal CTA), everything else is cream and dark green.

## Patterns
- **Illustrated intent-setting cards**: Using painterly, editorial illustrations instead of icons or photography to represent conversation modes — this softens the AI utility feel and creates emotional warmth before any interaction begins.
- **Verb-first card labels**: "Just vent," "Brainstorm ideas," "Practice a big conversation" — action-oriented, emotionally resonant language that frames the AI as a collaborator, not a tool.
- **Scroll-clipping as infinite signal**: The bottom row of cards is deliberately half-cropped, communicating more options exist without requiring a "see more" button.
- **Persistent input bar with dual modality**: Text input AND a voice call button coexist in the same bar — two interaction modes surfaced equally without hierarchy conflict.
- **No category labels or tabs**: The grid is flat — no filtering, no sections. Reduces cognitive load on a screen where emotional state is the entry point.
- **Illustration style as brand tone**: The consistent warm, slightly retro illustration aesthetic does heavy lifting — it signals "calm, thoughtful, human" before a word is read.

## Notes
- Serif type + cream bg = anti-tech aesthetic, intentional warmth signal
- Illustrations > icons when emotional context matters
- Verb-first labels as UX copywriting pattern worth stealing
- Dual-mode input bar (text + voice) without visual tension — study the balance
- Clipped bottom row = scroll affordance without UI chrome
- One accent color only — teal CTA pops precisely because nothing else competes
- Card image:text ratio (~55/45) feels editorial, not app-like — intentional
- Consider: what does your home screen's illustration style say about your product's emotional register?

## Screen
AI conversational assistant home screen — the entry/mode-selection screen before a chat session begins. Two-column card grid acts as a conversation starter menu. Sits at the top of the user flow, functioning as both a home screen and intent selector. Layout: large header, 2-col scrollable card grid, persistent bottom input bar.

## Layout & Spacing
2-column asymmetric card grid with generous gutters (~12px between cards). Cards have equal width but text labels vary in line count, creating natural height variation that feels editorial rather than rigid. Top header sits in generous whitespace above the grid. Bottom input bar is fixed/floating, slightly overlapping the scroll content to signal continuity. Cards appear to have ~16px internal padding between image and label text.

## Components
- **Mode-selection cards**: Rounded rectangle (~16px radius), cream/off-white background, illustration image top half, label text bottom half. No border, relies on background color contrast against page background.
- **Illustration images**: Cropped to fill top portion of card with rounded top corners only — painted/editorial illustration style, not photography or flat icons.
- **Header**: Large display text, left-aligned, with circular profile icon button (ghost/muted fill) top-right.
- **Bottom input bar**: Pill-shaped, cream fill, logo mark on left, placeholder text center-left, circular phone/voice icon button on right (dark green fill, contrasting CTA).
- **Profile button**: Circular, muted warm gray fill, person icon — low visual weight.

## Typography & Color
**Type hierarchy**: Display-weight serif or semi-serif for "Welcome to Pi" (large, ~32px, dark forest green). Card labels use a medium-weight serif in the same dark green, ~18–20px, left-aligned, multi-line allowed. Placeholder text in input bar is lighter weight, muted.

**Color palette**: 
- Background: warm cream/off-white (#F5F0E8 range)
- Primary text + UI: deep forest green (~#2D4A3E)
- Card backgrounds: slightly warmer cream, distinct from page bg
- CTA button: saturated dark green circle
- Illustrations: warm terracotta, sage, rust, muted naturalistic palette — cohesive across all cards

Color used functionally: green anchors all interactive and typographic elements; cream tones create softness; illustration warmth adds emotional texture without UI noise.

## Patterns
- **Illustrated intent selection**: Using hand-painted editorial illustrations instead of icons or abstract visuals to represent conversation modes — makes abstract AI use-cases feel emotionally tangible and human.
- **Soft mode-selection as onboarding**: Rather than asking "what do you want to do?" in text, the grid lets users self-identify with a vibe/scenario — lowers cognitive friction for open-ended AI tools.
- **Persistent input bar as escape hatch**: The "Talk with Pi" bar is always visible even on the home/selection screen — users don't have to pick a mode, they can just start talking. Removes commitment pressure.
- **Intentional scroll truncation**: Third row of cards is partially visible, signaling more options exist without a "see more" button — pure scroll affordance.
- **Illustration style as brand differentiator**: Consistent painted aesthetic across all cards creates a unified editorial feel that distances the product from sterile tech UI conventions — warmth as a trust signal.
- **Voice CTA elevated**: Phone/voice icon in the input bar is the highest-contrast element on screen — subtly prioritizes voice interaction over typing.

## Notes
- Illustrations > icons for emotional resonance in AI/companion apps
- Card label text allowed to wrap to 3 lines — don't force truncation, let it breathe
- Cream-on-cream card/background separation is subtle — works because of rounded corners + slight value shift
- "Just vent" as a label — copy tone matches illustration warmth, not feature-speak
- Voice button as primary CTA even on a text-input bar — consider what you're actually optimizing for
- No iconography anywhere except profile + voice — restraint pays off
- Scrollable grid with persistent floating bar = home + action always coexist

## Screen
Social discovery app — map-based people/activity feed. This is the main exploration screen, likely reached immediately after onboarding or from a home tab. Full-bleed map occupies the entire screen with UI elements floating on top. Primary interactions: tap user avatars, send message, call, browse virtual gifts dropped on map.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Full-bleed map as canvas — zero margin, zero padding. UI chrome floats as independent layers above the map. City label anchored top-left in large bold type acts as a location header. Bottom portion of map transitions into a gift/sticker discovery shelf (lower ~35% of screen). A persistent CTA bar (Message + Call) floats above the gift zone. Distance compass widget sits mid-left as an isolated floating element. No card containers or background panels — everything lives directly on the map surface.

---

## Components
- **User avatar pin (center):** Large circular photo crop (~64px), no border ring, sits directly on map — the "you are here" anchor
- **Secondary avatar pins:** Smaller circular crops (~32px) with subtle shadow, scattered across map
- **Anonymous user pin:** Black balaclava emoji-style icon — visual shorthand for hidden/anonymous user
- **Distance ring label:** Dotted circular text path reading "22M · 22M · 22M" orbiting the center avatar — radial distance indicator
- **Distance compass widget:** Pill/clock-face widget, white background, shows "7.2K mi" with directional tick marks — analog-inspired
- **3D gift stickers:** Large 3D rendered emoji-style objects (hearts, fire, poop, crying figure, eyes, beer, thumbs up) placed on map with label captions below — gift drop system
- **Message CTA:** Wide pill button, gold/yellow gradient fill, bold serif-adjacent label "Message" — dominant action
- **Call button:** Circular white button with phone icon, paired right of Message button
- **"4X" zoom indicator:** Bold display-weight text floating on map, indicates zoom multiplier

---

## Typography & Color
- **City label:** Extra-bold/black weight, ~32px, black — display hierarchy, feels editorial
- **Gift labels:** Small caption text below each 3D object, light gray, lowercase — subordinate metadata
- **"Message" button:** Medium weight, ~18px, dark text on gold — high contrast CTA
- **Distance ring:** Small curved path text, tight tracking, muted
- **Color palette:** Map uses warm beige/tan tones (muted street map). Gold/yellow for primary CTA. Purple accent on logo. Black for anonymous avatar. 3D stickers introduce full color spectrum as content, not UI chrome. Overall UI chrome is minimal — white, black, gold only.

---

## Patterns
- **Radial distance text path** — distance displayed as orbiting dotted text around the user's own pin rather than a static label. Spatial metaphor reinforces "you are the center"
- **3D object drops as social currency** — gifts rendered as large 3D objects placed geographically on the map with named labels, turning the map into a social artifact layer rather than pure navigation
- **Anonymous avatar as character** — masked figure icon communicates hidden identity without text, keeps visual language consistent with avatar system
- **Gold gradient pill as sole dominant CTA** — only one element uses color in the action layer, making the hierarchy unambiguous despite a visually noisy map background
- **Compass/distance widget with analog aesthetic** — tick-mark clock face for a distance readout is unexpected; borrows from physical instrument design to make abstract distance feel tangible
- **Map as social canvas, not utility** — the map is never used for navigation; it's purely a spatial social graph. POI labels from base map bleed through, adding city texture without being designed content
- **Dual action pairing (message + call)** — pill + circle button side-by-side creates visual rhythm; pill dominates, circle is secondary but immediately legible

---

## Notes
- Radial text path for proximity = steal this for any "you are here" social map
- 3D sticker drops on geo canvas → gifts as place-markers, not just notifications
- Gold CTA on map = color scarcity principle working hard
- Anonymous user needs a character, not just a blank avatar — masked icon solves this elegantly
- Analog instrument widget for digital distance metric = unexpected delight
- Full-bleed map + floating UI = no wasted chrome, everything earns its place
- Caption labels under 3D objects feel like museum exhibit tags — playful framing

## Screen
Travel discovery and planning app home screen. Entry point of the main app experience after onboarding. Vertical scroll layout with a fixed header, full-width search bar, a contextual location prompt banner, and two horizontally scrolling content carousels. Primary interactions: search, location permission grant, card saves (favorites), and carousel browsing.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Top-down hierarchy: logo/notification header → full-width search → location nudge banner → section 1 carousel → section 2 carousel. Generous vertical breathing room between sections (~24–32px gaps). Carousels bleed off the right edge intentionally to signal horizontal scrollability. Section headers are left-aligned with consistent left margin matching card edges, creating a clean vertical rhythm. The location banner sits in its own contained row with clear visual separation from both search and content below.

---

## Components
- **Search bar**: Pill-shaped, full-width, light stroke border, left-anchored search icon, muted placeholder text. Feels open and inviting rather than utilitarian.
- **Location permission banner**: Horizontal row with a large circular green icon (map pin with plus), two-line copy (headline + subtext), and a right-arrow chevron. Inline CTA that avoids a disruptive modal.
- **Destination cards (large)**: Tall portrait-ratio image cards with a dark gradient overlay at the bottom for text legibility. City name in large bold white type, country in smaller white subtext. Top-left floating pill tags (category labels, semi-transparent dark fill). Top-right heart/save icon in a white circle.
- **Trending cards (small)**: Shorter, wider cards, same heart icon treatment visible at top right. Grid-like but still horizontal scroll.
- **Section headers**: Bold, large sans-serif, left-aligned. No "see all" link visible — content implies scroll to explore.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Section headers ~22px bold → Card city name ~28px heavy/black weight → Card country ~14px regular → Banner headline ~16px semibold → Banner subtext ~13px regular gray
- **Color palette**: White background, near-black text for headers, medium gray for secondary text. Brand green used exclusively on the location icon circle — high contrast, draws the eye. Card text is white-on-dark-gradient. Tag pills use near-black semi-transparent fills with white text.
- **Color function**: Green is reserved solely for the location/action prompt — signals interactivity without overuse. No color used decoratively elsewhere; photography carries all visual warmth.

---

## Patterns
- **Inline permission request**: Location access is surfaced as a passive content row rather than a system-style modal interrupt. Lower friction, higher contextual relevance — user sees the value proposition (nearby results) before deciding.
- **Category tags on cards**: Floating pill labels on destination imagery serve dual purpose — they categorize content AND create a sense of editorial curation, making algorithmic content feel hand-picked.
- **Peek scrolling**: Cards are deliberately cropped at the right edge to communicate "more here" without any explicit affordance like dots or arrows. The partial card is a stronger scroll invitation than an indicator dot.
- **Save-on-browse**: Heart icon placed directly on cards in the scroll context (not inside a detail view) enables micro-commitment without navigation — reduces friction for wishlist building.
- **Two distinct content sections with different card sizes**: The size shift between "Plan your next adventure" (large portrait cards) and "Trending with travelers" (smaller landscape cards) creates visual rhythm and implies different content intent — aspirational vs. popular/social proof.
- **Photography as the entire UI**: Minimal chrome means destination imagery does all the emotional selling. UI elements are deliberately lightweight to not compete.

---

## Notes
- Inline permission nudge > modal — steal this pattern for any feature requiring sensitive access
- Green icon circle = single brand color anchor on an otherwise neutral screen — restraint works
- Bleed cards = scroll affordance, no dots needed
- Heart on card in feed = wishlist without leaving context
- Tag pills on imagery: editorial feel + taxonomy in one element
- Two section types, two card sizes = rhythm + hierarchy without extra chrome
- Heavy city name weight over gradient = legibility + drama simultaneously
- Search placeholder copy ("Places to go, things to do, hotels...") sets scope without overwhelming

## Screen
Travel discovery app home screen — the entry point of the main app experience. Serves as a browse/explore hub before a user has committed to a specific destination or trip. Layout flows vertically: header → global search → location prompt banner → editorial card carousel → trending section (partially visible). Primary interactive elements: search bar, location access CTA, horizontally scrollable destination cards, heart/save buttons, bottom nav.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Clean vertical rhythm with generous section padding between modules (~24–32px). Each content section is clearly delineated without dividers — whitespace alone does the separating. The horizontal card carousels bleed off the right edge intentionally, signaling scrollability. The location prompt sits in its own contained row with breathing room above and below, keeping it distinct from both search and content sections. Left-aligned section headers anchor the content grid.

---

## Components
- **Global search bar** — full-width pill shape, light stroke border, left-aligned search icon + placeholder text. Soft and inviting rather than aggressive.
- **Location access banner** — inline row component: circular green icon container (brand color fill) + two-line text block + right chevron arrow. Feels like a list item, not a modal interrupt.
- **Destination cards (large)** — tall portrait-ratio image cards with full-bleed photography. City name in large bold white type bottom-left, country name in smaller weight below. Category tags (pill chips) overlay top-left. Heart icon button top-right in white circle container.
- **Heart/save button** — white circular button with outline heart icon, floats over imagery. Appears on both large and small cards.
- **Category tag chips** — small white rounded pill labels overlaid on card imagery, semi-transparent or solid white fill.
- **Section headers** — large, bold, left-aligned text acting as editorial titles.

---

## Typography & Color
Strong two-level hierarchy: section titles are large (~22px) bold sans-serif in near-black; card destination names are even larger (~28–32px) bold white for legibility over imagery; supporting text (country, subtitles) drops to regular weight, smaller size. Location banner uses medium-weight heading + light subtext.

Color palette is restrained: white background, near-black text, single brand green used only for the location icon circle. Photography carries all the color weight. Functional use of color: green = location/action, white = overlaid UI on dark imagery, black text on white for all editorial content.

---

## Patterns

**Soft permission request inline** — Location access is surfaced as a passive inline banner (not a system modal or interstitial). It uses a friendly question headline + explanatory subtext + chevron. This reduces friction and avoids the jarring native permission dialog until the user opts in by tapping. Smart deferral of a high-stakes ask.

**Editorial framing of algorithmic content** — "Plan your next adventure" and "Trending with travelers" are editorial-sounding section titles that make curated/algorithmic content feel handpicked. Adds warmth and intentionality to what could feel like a data feed.

**Category tags as discovery metadata on cards** — Overlaying "Cultural Tours" and "After dark" chips on destination imagery adds a secondary layer of intent-matching without requiring a separate filter step. Users can scan content type at a glance while browsing visually.

**Peek-scroll affordance** — The second card is deliberately cropped at the right edge, communicating horizontal scroll without any explicit instruction or indicator dots. The partial card creates visual tension that invites interaction.

**Hierarchy inversion on cards** — Destination name is the largest text element on the card, larger than section headers. This prioritizes place identity over UI chrome, reinforcing the aspirational/emotional nature of travel content.

**Save-in-context** — Heart buttons are embedded directly on browse cards rather than inside a detail view, enabling wishlist behavior without breaking the discovery flow.

---

## Notes
- Inline permission ask > modal interrupt — use this pattern whenever possible for optional access requests
- Green used sparingly = single accent color does more work
- Photography IS the UI — minimal chrome lets imagery carry emotional weight
- Bleed-off-edge carousel = scroll affordance without dots/arrows
- Category chips on cards = filter metadata surfaced at browse layer, not just in search
- "Plan your next adventure" — editorial voice makes feeds feel curated
- Large bold city name on card = place as hero, not feature list
- Heart on card = save without leaving flow — reduce friction to wishlist

## Screen
Multi-currency digital wallet home screen. Fintech app, likely a travel money or international payments product. This is the primary dashboard — the first screen after login. Layout flows top-to-bottom: account switcher → quick actions → recently paid contacts → transaction history → bottom nav.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is divided into two clear white card sections separated by a light gray background gap — a deliberate visual "break" that groups wallet actions above and transaction history below. The wallet selector row sits outside these cards, anchored near the top. Quick action tiles use a 3-column horizontal scroll. Generous vertical spacing between section headers and content creates breathing room without feeling sparse.

## Components
- **Wallet selector pills**: Rounded rectangle chips with flag emoji + currency code + balance. Active/selected state implied by the dark filled circle (app logo) to the left. An "Add wallet" pill with a + icon extends the row, hinting at scrollability.
- **Quick action tiles**: Square cards with bold background colors (lime green, yellow, purple), 3D illustrated icons, and short action labels in white/dark text at the bottom-left. Rounded corners, no border.
- **"View all" buttons**: Small pill-shaped buttons with light gray fill — subtle, secondary, non-competing with section headers.
- **Transaction rows**: Three-column layout — icon/avatar left, merchant name + timestamp center, amount right. Currency conversion transactions show two-line amounts (debit in black, credit in green below).
- **Bottom nav**: Icon + label, minimal weight icons, star/sparkle icon for Rewards tab adds personality.

## Typography & Color
Strong type hierarchy: section headers ("Recently paid," "Transactions") are bold and large (~18px). Transaction merchant names are medium weight, timestamps are small and gray. Amounts are right-aligned, bold — negative in near-black, positive in green.

Color palette: White base, light gray background, black for primary text. Accent colors confined entirely to the quick action tiles (lime, yellow, purple) — nowhere else, which keeps the rest of the UI calm. Green used exclusively for positive monetary values — functional color coding.

## Patterns
- **Multi-wallet switcher as horizontal chip row** rather than a dropdown — keeps all balances visible at a glance, reduces taps to switch context. The "Add wallet" chip inline with existing wallets is a smart progressive disclosure pattern.
- **3D illustrated icons on action tiles** give the cards visual weight and memorability without relying on text — each tile is immediately scannable by illustration alone.
- **Dual-currency transaction display**: Showing both the GBP debit and EUR credit on a single transaction row communicates the conversion clearly without requiring a detail screen tap. The green secondary amount signals gain/receipt.
- **Section card separation via background color gap** (not a divider line) — creates a stronger visual hierarchy break than a hairline rule would.
- **Privacy lock icon** in the top-left corner of the outer frame (outside the phone mockup) suggests a screenshot-mode privacy toggle — an interesting meta-UI pattern for redacting sensitive data in demos.
- **Blurred/redacted content** in "Recently paid" and one transaction row — intentional privacy masking baked into the design system, not an afterthought.

## Notes
- Wallet chips as scannable balance summary — no need to tap in
- Color-block action tiles = wayfinding by color, not just label
- Dual-line amount for FX conversions — show both sides of the trade inline
- Gray background gap as section divider > hairline rules
- Green = money in, black = money out — zero ambiguity
- "Add wallet" as inline chip keeps discoverability without a modal
- Privacy/redact mode as a first-class UI state worth designing for
- 3D isometric illustrations on CTA tiles add premium feel cheaply
